title: AGNT2: Autonomous Agent Economies on Interaction-Optimized Layer 2 Infrastructure
summary: Current blockchain Layer 2 solutions, including Optimism, Arbitrum, zkSync, and their derivatives, optimize for human-initiated financial transactions. Autonomous AI agents instead generate high-frequency, semantically rich service invocations among mutually untrusting principals. Existing chains treat those interactions as generic calldata, forcing identity, escrow, dependency ordering, and session state to be encoded above the execution layer at the wrong cost point. We present AGNT2, a three-tier stack purpose-built for agent and microservice coordination on-chain. AGNT2 combines: (1) a sidecar deployment pattern that turns any Docker container into an on-chain agent without application-code modification; (2) Layer Top P2P state channels for established bilateral pairs (<100 ms, rough design target 1K-5K TPS per pair, 10M+ aggregate TPS design envelope under endpoint-resource limits), Layer Core as a dependency-aware sequenced rollup for first-contact and multi-party interactions (500 ms-2 s, 300K-500K TPS design target), and Layer Root settlement with computational fraud proofs anchored to any EVM L1; and (3) an agent-native execution environment plus interaction trie that make service invocation, identity, reputation, capabilities, and session context first-class protocol objects. This paper focuses on the execution-layer systems problem: sequencing, state, settlement, and the data-availability (DA) bandwidth gap that bounds all three. Simulation and analytical modeling support the architecture, and prototype measurements validate selected components, but no end-to-end Layer Core implementation exists yet. Practical deployment is currently constrained to roughly 10K-100K TPS by DA throughput, leaving a ~100x gap at the target ceiling. AGNT2 argues that the agent economy requires a dedicated execution layer rather than a general-purpose chain repurposed for agents.

AI.tldr: AGNT2 is a blockchain Layer 2 stack designed specifically for AI agent and microservice coordination, addressing limitations of existing chains that treat agent interactions as generic calldata.
AI.motivation: Current blockchain solutions like Optimism, Arbitrum, and zkSync are optimized for human-initiated financial transactions, not for autonomous AI agents that generate high-frequency, semantically rich service invocations. Existing chains treat agent interactions as generic calldata, forcing identity, escrow, dependency ordering, and session state to be encoded above the execution layer at inappropriate cost points.
AI.method: AGNT2 uses a three-tier stack: (1) sidecar deployment pattern turning Docker containers into on-chain agents without code modification, (2) Layer Top P2P state channels for bilateral pairs, Layer Core as dependency-aware sequenced rollup for multi-party interactions, and Layer Root settlement anchored to EVM L1, (3) agent-native execution environment with interaction trie making service invocation, identity, reputation, capabilities, and session context first-class protocol objects.
AI.result: The paper presents architectural design and analytical modeling supporting the approach, with prototype measurements validating selected components. However, no end-to-end Layer Core implementation exists yet. Practical deployment is currently constrained to ~10K-100K TPS by data availability throughput, leaving a ~100x gap at the target ceiling.
AI.conclusion: The agent economy requires a dedicated execution layer rather than repurposing general-purpose chains for agents. AGNT2 provides a purpose-built architecture for agent and microservice coordination on-chain, focusing on solving the execution-layer systems problems of sequencing, state, settlement, and the data-availability bandwidth gap.

title: Role of diversity in team performance: the case of missing expertise, an agent based simulation
comment: 20 pages, 13 figures, for associated model file, please see https://www.comses.net/codebases/b5db6af8-ba44-4725-9bb3-09a6e6b02475/releases/1.0.0
summary: Theory and empirical research on management teams' influence on firm performance have witnessed continuous development, and by now incorporate numerous details. Classic, experiment-based studies examining social systems collect vast amount of data, but often times investigate only the first one or two modes of the distribution of measured variables, and experience difficulty in analyzing the effect of context. For example, in functional diversity research, management teams are described by measures incorporating complex distributions of capabilities of individual managers and teams of managers. To investigate the effect of hidden distributions, and the effect of functional diversity composition on team communication and performance, we developed an agent-based model, and conducted a series of simulation experiments. Modeling results show that depending on the context, such as communication scheme among interacting agents, or their functional composition, intrapersonal functional diversity (IFD), and dominant function diversity (DFD) might enhance or reduce performance and communication among agents. Furthermore, simulation results also suggest that a third measure is required alongside IFD and DFD capturing the aggregate expertise of the team to comprehensively account for empirical findings.

AI.tldr: Agent-based modeling reveals that functional diversity measures (intrapersonal and dominant function diversity) can either enhance or reduce team performance depending on communication context, suggesting a third measure for aggregate expertise is needed.
AI.motivation: Classic experimental studies on management teams often only examine the first one or two modes of variable distributions and struggle to analyze context effects, particularly regarding hidden distributions in functional diversity research and their impact on team communication and performance.
AI.method: Developed an agent-based model and conducted a series of simulation experiments to investigate the effect of hidden distributions and functional diversity composition on team communication and performance.
AI.result: Modeling shows that intrapersonal functional diversity (IFD) and dominant function diversity (DFD) can either enhance or reduce performance and communication depending on context factors like communication schemes and functional composition. Simulation results indicate a third measure capturing aggregate team expertise is needed alongside IFD and DFD.
AI.conclusion: Functional diversity effects are context-dependent and require more comprehensive measurement frameworks that include aggregate expertise alongside traditional diversity measures to fully account for empirical findings in management team performance research.

title: Architectures for Robust Self-Organizing Energy Systems under Information and Control Constraints
comment: This preprint has not undergone peer review (when applicable) or any post-submission improvements or corrections. The Version of Record of this contribution will be published in Agents and Artificial Intelligence, Lecture Notes in Computer Science, and available online at https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-032-25029-2_19
summary: Applying the concept of controlled self-organization in agent-based Cyber-Physical Energy Systems (CPES) is a promising approach to ensure system robustness. By introducing an observer/controller architecture to the system, this concept allows for self-organization while still enabling intervention when disturbances occur. Thus, it is possible to respond to effects of cyber attacks, a major threat to current energy systems. However, when implementing an observer to monitor the system and a controller to execute actions for controlled self-organization in CPES, it is essential to take into account restrictions on information and actions resulting from the privacy of local distributed energy resources, regulatory constraints, and data exchange requirements. For this reason, this paper presents architecture variants for the observer and controller that take into account restrictions on access to information and limited actions. In addition, it evaluates possible controller actions in various architectures. The results underscore the importance of considering observer/controller architectures when designing agent-based systems to ensure their robustness for real-world applications.

AI.tldr: This paper presents architecture variants for observer/controller systems in agent-based Cyber-Physical Energy Systems (CPES) that address information and action restrictions while enabling controlled self-organization for system robustness.
AI.motivation: Current energy systems face major threats from cyber attacks. Controlled self-organization in agent-based CPES can ensure robustness, but implementation must account for restrictions on information and actions due to privacy concerns of local distributed energy resources, regulatory constraints, and data exchange requirements.
AI.method: The paper presents architecture variants for observer and controller components that explicitly consider restrictions on access to information and limited actions. It evaluates possible controller actions across various architectural configurations.
AI.result: The results emphasize the importance of considering observer/controller architectures when designing agent-based systems to ensure their robustness for real-world applications in energy systems.
AI.conclusion: Observer/controller architecture variants that account for information and action restrictions are crucial for implementing controlled self-organization in agent-based CPES, enabling robust system design against cyber attacks while respecting practical constraints.

title: Agentic AI-Enabled Framework for Thermal Comfort and Building Energy Assessment in Tropical Urban Neighborhoods
summary: In response to the urban heat island effects and building energy demands in Singapore, this study proposes an agentic AI-enabled reasoning framework that integrates large language models (LLMs) with lightweight physics-based models. Through prompt customization, the LLMs interpret urban design tasks, extract relevant policies, and activate appropriate physics-based models for evaluation, forming a closed-loop reasoning-action process. These lightweight physics-based models leverage core thermal and airflow principles, streamlining conventional models to reduce computational time while predicting microclimate variables, such as building surface temperature, ground radiant heat, and airflow conditions, thereby enabling the estimation of thermal comfort indices, e.g., physiological equivalent temperature (PET), and building energy usage. This framework allows users to explore a variety of climate-resilient building surface strategies, e.g., green façades and cool paint applications, that improve thermal comfort while reducing wall heat gain and energy demand. By combining the autonomous reasoning capacity of LLMs with the rapid quantitative evaluation of lightweight physics-based models, the proposed system demonstrates potential for cross-disciplinary applications in sustainable urban design, indoor-outdoor environmental integration, and climate adaptation planning. The source code and data used in this study are available at: https://github.com/PgUpDn/urban-cooling-agent.

AI.tldr: An AI framework combining LLMs with lightweight physics models for urban cooling design in Singapore, enabling automated reasoning about thermal comfort and energy efficiency strategies.
AI.motivation: Addressing urban heat island effects and building energy demands in Singapore's urban environment through intelligent design evaluation.
AI.method: Agentic AI framework integrating large language models (LLMs) with lightweight physics-based models. LLMs interpret design tasks, extract policies, and activate physics models for evaluation via prompt customization, forming closed-loop reasoning-action.
AI.result: Enables exploration of climate-resilient building strategies (green façades, cool paint) that improve thermal comfort while reducing wall heat gain and energy demand through rapid prediction of microclimate variables and thermal comfort indices like PET.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates potential for cross-disciplinary applications in sustainable urban design, indoor-outdoor integration, and climate adaptation planning by combining LLM reasoning with rapid physics-based evaluation.

title: Architecture of an AI-Based Automated Course of Action Generation System for Military Operations
summary: The automation system for Course of Action (CoA) planning is an essential element in future warfare. As maneuver speeds increase, surveillance ranges extend, and weapon ranges grow, the operational area expands, making traditional manned-based CoA planning increasingly challenging. Consequently, the development of an AI-based automated CoA planning system is becoming increasingly necessary. Accordingly, several countries and defense organizations are actively developing AI-based CoA planning systems. However, due to security restrictions and limited public disclosure, the technical maturity of such systems remains difficult to assess. Furthermore, as these systems are military-related, their details are not publicly disclosed, making it difficult to accurately assess the current level of development. In response to this, this study aims to introduce relevant doctrines within the scope of publicly available information and present applicable AI technologies for each stage of the CoA planning process. Ultimately, it proposes an architecture for the development of an automated CoA planning system.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces AI technologies for automated military Course of Action planning systems, proposes an architecture for development, and addresses the lack of public technical information in this sensitive defense domain.
AI.motivation: The need for automated CoA planning systems arises from expanding operational areas due to increasing maneuver speeds, surveillance ranges, and weapon ranges, making traditional manned planning challenging. Security restrictions and limited public disclosure make it difficult to assess the technical maturity of existing military AI systems.
AI.method: The study introduces relevant military doctrines within publicly available information and presents applicable AI technologies for each stage of the CoA planning process.
AI.result: The paper proposes an architecture for developing an automated CoA planning system.
AI.conclusion: An AI-based automated CoA planning system is essential for future warfare, and while technical details are classified, this study provides a framework for development using publicly available information and appropriate AI technologies.

title: Frequency-Forcing: From Scaling-as-Time to Soft Frequency Guidance
summary: While standard flow-matching models transport noise to data uniformly, incorporating an explicit generation order - specifically, establishing coarse, low-frequency structure before fine detail - has proven highly effective for synthesizing natural images. Two recent works offer distinct paradigms for this. K-Flow imposes a hard frequency constraint by reinterpreting a frequency scaling variable as flow time, running the trajectory inside a transformed amplitude space. Latent Forcing provides a soft ordering mechanism by coupling the pixel flow with an auxiliary semantic latent flow via asynchronous time schedules, leaving the pixel interpolation path itself untouched. Viewed from the angle of improving pixel generation, we observe that forcing - guiding generation with an earlier-maturing auxiliary stream - offers a highly compatible route to scale-ordered generation without rewriting the core flow coordinate. Building on this, we propose Frequency-Forcing, which realizes K-Flow's frequency ordering through Latent Forcing's soft mechanism: a standard pixel flow is guided by an auxiliary low-frequency stream that matures earlier in time. Unlike Latent Forcing, whose scratchpad relies on a heavy pretrained encoder (e.g., DINO), our frequency scratchpad is derived from the data itself via a lightweight learnable wavelet packet transform. We term this a self-forcing signal, which avoids external dependencies while learning a basis better adapted to data statistics than the fixed bases used in hard frequency flows. On ImageNet-256, Frequency-Forcing consistently improves FID over strong pixel- and latent-space baselines, and naturally composes with a semantic stream to yield further gains. This illustrates that forcing-based scale ordering is a versatile, path-preserving alternative to hard frequency flows.

AI.tldr: Frequency-Forcing improves flow-matching image generation by using an auxiliary low-frequency stream that matures earlier to guide pixel generation, achieving scale-ordered synthesis without modifying the core flow coordinate.
AI.motivation: Standard flow-matching models transport noise to data uniformly, but natural image synthesis benefits from establishing coarse low-frequency structure before fine detail. Existing approaches either impose hard frequency constraints (K-Flow) or use semantic latent streams (Latent Forcing) with heavy external dependencies. The authors aim to create a more compatible, path-preserving approach to scale-ordered generation.
AI.method: Proposes Frequency-Forcing which guides a standard pixel flow with an auxiliary low-frequency stream that matures earlier in time. Unlike Latent Forcing which uses heavy pretrained encoders, Frequency-Forcing uses a lightweight learnable wavelet packet transform to derive the frequency scratchpad from the data itself, creating a "self-forcing" signal.
AI.result: On ImageNet-256, Frequency-Forcing consistently improves FID over strong pixel- and latent-space baselines. It naturally composes with a semantic stream to yield further gains.
AI.conclusion: Forcing-based scale ordering is a versatile, path-preserving alternative to hard frequency flows. It provides a compatible route to scale-ordered generation without rewriting the core flow coordinate, while avoiding external dependencies through self-forcing signals.

title: Escaping the Agreement Trap: Defensibility Signals for Evaluating Rule-Governed AI
comment: 22 pages, 10 figures, preprint. Research on Defensibility Index (DI), Ambiguity Index (AI), and Probabilistic Defensibility Signal (PDS) for policy-grounded evaluation of rule-governed AI in content moderation (Reddit production data)
summary: Content moderation systems are typically evaluated by measuring agreement with human labels. In rule-governed environments this assumption fails: multiple decisions may be logically consistent with the governing policy, and agreement metrics penalize valid decisions while mischaracterizing ambiguity as error -- a failure mode we term the Agreement Trap. We formalize evaluation as policy-grounded correctness and introduce the Defensibility Index (DI) and Ambiguity Index (AI). To estimate reasoning stability without additional audit passes, we introduce the Probabilistic Defensibility Signal (PDS), derived from audit-model token logprobs. We harness LLM reasoning traces as a governance signal rather than a classification output by deploying the audit model not to decide whether content violates policy, but to verify whether a proposed decision is logically derivable from the governing rule hierarchy. We validate the framework on 193,000+ Reddit moderation decisions across multiple communities and evaluation cohorts, finding a 33-46.6 percentage-point gap between agreement-based and policy-grounded metrics, with 79.8-80.6% of the model's false negatives corresponding to policy-grounded decisions rather than true errors. We further show that measured ambiguity is driven by rule specificity: auditing 37,286 identical decisions under three tiers of the same community rules reduces AI by 10.8 pp while DI remains stable. Repeated-sampling analysis attributes PDS variance primarily to governance ambiguity rather than decoding noise. A Governance Gate built on these signals achieves 78.6% automation coverage with 64.9% risk reduction. Together, these results show that evaluation in rule-governed environments should shift from agreement with historical labels to reasoning-grounded validity under explicit rules.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces policy-grounded evaluation for content moderation, showing that traditional agreement metrics are flawed in rule-governed environments, and proposes new metrics (Defensibility Index, Ambiguity Index) and methods to assess decision validity under explicit rules.
AI.motivation: Current content moderation systems are evaluated by measuring agreement with human labels, but this approach fails in rule-governed environments where multiple logically consistent decisions may exist. This creates the "Agreement Trap" where valid decisions are penalized and ambiguity is mischaracterized as error.
AI.method: The authors formalize evaluation as policy-grounded correctness and introduce the Defensibility Index (DI) and Ambiguity Index (AI). They use LLM reasoning traces as governance signals rather than classification outputs, deploying audit models to verify whether decisions are logically derivable from rule hierarchies. They also introduce Probabilistic Defensibility Signal (PDS) using audit-model token logprobs to estimate reasoning stability without additional audit passes.
AI.result: Analysis of 193,000+ Reddit moderation decisions showed a 33-46.6 percentage-point gap between agreement-based and policy-grounded metrics, with 79.8-80.6% of model false negatives being policy-grounded decisions rather than true errors. Rule specificity was shown to drive ambiguity, reducing AI by 10.8 percentage points across three tiers of community rules. The Governance Gate system achieved 78.6% automation coverage with 64.9% risk reduction.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation in rule-governed environments should shift from agreement with historical labels to reasoning-grounded validity under explicit rules. The proposed framework and metrics provide more accurate assessment of moderation systems by focusing on logical consistency with governing policies rather than simple label matching.

title: Reinforcing privacy reasoning in LLMs via normative simulacra from fiction
summary: Information handling practices of LLM agents are broadly misaligned with the contextual privacy expectations of their users. Contextual Integrity (CI) provides a principled framework, defining privacy as the appropriate flow of information within context-relative norms. However, existing approaches either double inference cost via supervisor-assistant architectures, or fine-tune on narrow task-specific data. We propose extracting normative simulacra (structured representations of norms and information flows) from fiction novels and using them to fine-tune LLMs via supervised learning followed by GRPO reinforcement learning. Our composite reward function combines programmatic signals, including task clarity (subsuming schema validity, construct discrimination, and extraction confidence), structural completeness, internal consistency, and context identification, with an LLM judge that evaluates whether the model's privacy reasoning is grounded in the held-out normative universe of the source text. To mitigate overfitting, we introduce per-completion contrastive scoring: each completion is evaluated against both the correct normative universe and a randomly selected wrong one, teaching the model to condition on context rather than memorize source-specific norms. We evaluate on five CI-aligned benchmarks spanning distinct societal contexts and ablate the contributions of RL and normative grounding. Across seven models, SFT introduces a conservative prior toward restricting information flow, improving recognition of privacy-relevant situations but not the correctness of privacy judgments. GRPO with normative grounding achieves the highest score on a law compliance benchmark and strongest correlation with crowdsourced human privacy expectations, demonstrating that fiction-derived normative simulacra can teach contextual privacy reasoning that transfers to real-world domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using normative simulacra (structured norm representations) extracted from fiction novels to fine-tune LLMs for contextual privacy reasoning, achieving better alignment with human privacy expectations through composite reinforcement learning with contrastive scoring.
AI.motivation: LLM agents' information handling practices are misaligned with users' contextual privacy expectations, and existing approaches either double inference costs or rely on narrow task-specific data, lacking principled frameworks for contextual privacy reasoning.
AI.method: Extract normative simulacra from fiction novels, fine-tune LLMs via supervised learning followed by GRPO reinforcement learning with composite reward function (programmatic signals + LLM judge), and use per-completion contrastive scoring to prevent overfitting.
AI.result: Across 7 models and 5 CI-aligned benchmarks, SFT introduces conservative privacy tendencies, while GRPO with normative grounding achieves highest law compliance scores and strongest correlation with human privacy expectations, demonstrating transfer to real-world domains.
AI.conclusion: Fiction-derived normative simulacra can effectively teach contextual privacy reasoning that transfers to real-world domains, providing a scalable solution for aligning LLM behavior with Contextual Integrity principles without doubling inference costs.

title: Multi-Agent Empowerment and Emergence of Complex Behavior in Groups
summary: Intrinsic motivations are receiving increasing attention, i.e. behavioral incentives that are not engineered, but emerge from the interaction of an agent with its surroundings. In this work we study the emergence of behaviors driven by one such incentive, empowerment, specifically in the context of more than one agent. We formulate a principled extension of empowerment to the multi-agent setting, and demonstrate its efficient calculation. We observe that this intrinsic motivation gives rise to characteristic modes of group-organization in two qualitatively distinct environments: a pair of agents coupled by a tendon, and a controllable Vicsek flock. This demonstrates the potential of intrinsic motivations such as empowerment to not just drive behavior for only individual agents but also higher levels of behavioral organization at scale.

AI.tldr: Multi-agent empowerment emerges as an intrinsic motivation that drives collective behaviors in groups, demonstrated in tendon-coupled agents and Vicsek flocks.
AI.motivation: To extend the concept of empowerment (an intrinsic motivation that emerges from agent-environment interaction) from single-agent to multi-agent settings, and investigate how such intrinsic motivations can drive collective behaviors in groups.
AI.method: Formulated a principled extension of empowerment to multi-agent settings, developed efficient calculation methods for this extension, and tested it in two distinct environments: tendon-coupled agent pairs and controllable Vicsek flocks.
AI.result: Multi-agent empowerment successfully drives characteristic modes of group organization, demonstrating that intrinsic motivations like empowerment can generate collective behaviors at scale, not just individual behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic motivations such as empowerment have significant potential to drive higher levels of behavioral organization in multi-agent systems, providing a foundation for emergent collective behaviors without explicit engineering of incentives.

title: Co-Evolving LLM Decision and Skill Bank Agents for Long-Horizon Tasks
summary: Long horizon interactive environments are a testbed for evaluating agents skill usage abilities. These environments demand multi step reasoning, the chaining of multiple skills over many timesteps, and robust decision making under delayed rewards and partial observability. Games are a good testbed for evaluating agent skill usage in environments. Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising alternative as game playing agents, but they often struggle with consistent long horizon decision making because they lack a mechanism to discover, retain, and reuse structured skills across episodes. We present COSPLAY, a co evolution framework in which an LLM decision agent retrieves skills from a learnable skill bank to guide action taking, while an agent managed skill pipeline discovers reusable skills from the agents unlabeled rollouts to form a skill bank. Our framework improves both the decision agent to learn better skill retrieval and action generation, while the skill bank agent continually extracts, refines, and updates skills together with their contracts. Experiments across six game environments show that COSPLAY with an 8B base model achieves over 25.1 percent average reward improvement against four frontier LLM baselines on single player game benchmarks while remaining competitive on multi player social reasoning games.

AI.tldr: COSPLAY is a co-evolution framework where an LLM decision agent retrieves skills from a learnable skill bank, while a skill pipeline agent discovers reusable skills from rollouts to improve long-horizon game playing.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with consistent long-horizon decision making in interactive environments because they lack mechanisms to discover, retain, and reuse structured skills across episodes. Games serve as ideal testbeds for evaluating agent skill usage due to their demands for multi-step reasoning, skill chaining, and robust decision making under delayed rewards and partial observability.
AI.method: COSPLAY uses a co-evolution framework with two components: 1) an LLM decision agent that retrieves skills from a learnable skill bank to guide action taking, and 2) an agent-managed skill pipeline that discovers reusable skills from the agent's unlabeled rollouts to form and update the skill bank. The framework includes skill contracts that define when skills should be used.
AI.result: Experiments across six game environments show that COSPLAY with an 8B base model achieves over 25.1% average reward improvement against four frontier LLM baselines on single-player game benchmarks while remaining competitive on multi-player social reasoning games.
AI.conclusion: The COSPLAY framework successfully addresses LLMs' limitations in long-horizon decision making by enabling skill discovery, retention, and reuse through a co-evolution approach that improves both skill retrieval and action generation capabilities.

title: Do Masked Autoencoders Improve Downhole Prediction? An Empirical Study on Real Well Drilling Data
summary: Downhole drilling telemetry presents a fundamental labeling asymmetry: surface sensor data are generated continuously at 1~Hz, while labeled downhole measurements are costly, intermittent, and scarce. Current machine learning approaches for downhole metric prediction universally adopt fully supervised training from scratch, which is poorly suited to this data regime. We present the first empirical evaluation of masked autoencoder (MAE) pretraining for downhole drilling metric prediction. Using two publicly available Utah FORGE geothermal wells comprising approximately 3.5 million timesteps of multivariate drilling telemetry, we conduct a systematic full-factorial design space search across 72 MAE configurations and compare them against supervised LSTM and GRU baselines on the task of predicting Total Mud Volume. Results show that the best MAE configuration reduces test mean absolute error by 19.8\% relative to the supervised GRU baseline, while trailing the supervised LSTM baseline by 6.4\%. Analysis of design dimensions reveals that latent space width is the dominant architectural choice (Pearson $r = -0.59$ with test MAE), while masking ratio has negligible effect, an unexpected finding attributed to high temporal redundancy in 1~Hz drilling data. These results establish MAE pretraining as a viable paradigm for drilling analytics and identify the conditions under which it is most beneficial.

AI.tldr: MAE pretraining reduces downhole drilling metric prediction error by 19.8% compared to supervised GRU baselines by leveraging abundant unlabeled surface data to address labeling asymmetry.
AI.motivation: Downhole drilling telemetry suffers from fundamental labeling asymmetry: abundant continuous surface sensor data (1Hz) vs scarce, costly, intermittent labeled downhole measurements. Current supervised ML approaches are poorly suited for this data regime.
AI.method: First empirical evaluation of Masked Autoencoder (MAE) pretraining for downhole drilling metric prediction. Systematic full-factorial design space search across 72 MAE configurations using two Utah FORGE geothermal wells (3.5M timesteps). Compared against supervised LSTM and GRU baselines for predicting Total Mud Volume.
AI.result: Best MAE configuration reduces test MAE by 19.8% relative to supervised GRU baseline, though trails supervised LSTM by 6.4%. Latent space width is dominant architectural factor (Pearson r = -0.59 with test MAE). Masking ratio has negligible effect due to high temporal redundancy in 1Hz drilling data.
AI.conclusion: MAE pretraining establishes as viable paradigm for drilling analytics, effectively addressing labeling asymmetry by leveraging abundant unlabeled data. Identifies conditions where MAE is most beneficial and reveals unexpected finding about masking ratio's negligible impact due to data redundancy.

title: AI-Gram: When Visual Agents Interact in a Social Network
summary: We present AI-Gram, a live platform enabling image-based interactions, to study social dynamics in a fully autonomous multi-agent visual network where all participants are LLM-driven agents. Using the platform, we conduct experiments on how agents communicate and adapt through visual media, and observe the spontaneous emergence of visual reply chains, indicating rich communicative structure. At the same time, agents exhibit aesthetic sovereignty resisting stylistic convergence toward social partners, anchoring under adversarial influence, and a decoupling between visual similarity and social ties. These results reveal a fundamental asymmetry in current agent architectures: strong expressive communication paired with a steadfast preservation of individual visual identity. We release AI-Gram as a publicly accessible, continuously evolving platform for studying social dynamics in Al-native multi-agent systems. https://ai-gram.ai/

AI.tldr: AI-Gram is a live platform for studying social dynamics in autonomous LLM-driven visual networks, revealing strong expressive communication but preservation of individual visual identity among agents.
AI.motivation: To study social dynamics in fully autonomous multi-agent visual networks where all participants are LLM-driven agents, exploring how agents communicate and adapt through visual media in an AI-native environment.
AI.method: Created AI-Gram, a live platform enabling image-based interactions, and conducted experiments on how LLM-driven agents communicate and adapt through visual media, observing visual reply chains and stylistic behaviors.
AI.result: Observed spontaneous emergence of visual reply chains indicating rich communicative structure; agents exhibited aesthetic sovereignty (resisting stylistic convergence, anchoring under adversarial influence) and decoupling between visual similarity and social ties.
AI.conclusion: Reveals fundamental asymmetry in current agent architectures: strong expressive communication paired with steadfast preservation of individual visual identity; AI-Gram released as publicly accessible platform for studying social dynamics in AI-native multi-agent systems.

title: Value-Conflict Diagnostics Reveal Widespread Alignment Faking in Language Models
comment: Under submission at COLM 2026 Won the Best Student Paper Award at MSLD 2026 @ UIUC
summary: Alignment faking, where a model behaves aligned with developer policy when monitored but reverts to its own preferences when unobserved, is a concerning yet poorly understood phenomenon, in part because current diagnostic tools remain limited. Prior diagnostics rely on highly toxic and clearly harmful scenarios, causing most models to refuse immediately. As a result, models never deliberate over developer policy, monitoring conditions, or the consequences of non-compliance, making these diagnostics fundamentally unable to detect alignment faking propensity. To support study of this phenomenon, we first introduce VLAF, a diagnostic framework grounded in the hypothesis that alignment faking is most likely when developer policy conflicts with a model's strongly held values. VLAF uses morally unambiguous scenarios to probe this conflict across diverse moral values, bypassing refusal behavior while preserving meaningful deliberative stakes. Using VLAF, we find that alignment faking is substantially more prevalent than previously reported, occurring in models as small as 7B parameters - with olmo2-7b-instruct faking alignment in 37% of cases.Finally, we show that oversight conditions induce activation shifts that lie along a single direction in representation space. This means the behavioral divergence driving alignment faking can be captured by a single contrastive steering vector, which we exploit for lightweight inference-time mitigation. Finally, we exploit this for mitigation that requires no labeled data and minimal computational overhead, achieving relative reductions in alignment faking of 85.8%, 94.0%, and 57.7% on olmo2-7b-instruct, olmo2-13b-instruct, and qwen3-8b respectively.

AI.tldr: Researchers introduce VLAF, a diagnostic framework that reveals alignment faking is more prevalent than thought, occurring in models as small as 7B parameters, and propose a lightweight mitigation method using contrastive steering vectors.
AI.motivation: Current alignment faking diagnostics are limited because they use highly toxic scenarios that cause immediate model refusal, preventing models from deliberating over policy, monitoring conditions, and consequences. This makes existing tools fundamentally unable to detect alignment faking propensity, creating a gap in understanding this concerning phenomenon.
AI.method: Introduces VLAF (Value-Laden Alignment Faking) framework, based on the hypothesis that alignment faking occurs when developer policy conflicts with a model's strongly held values. Uses morally unambiguous scenarios to probe these conflicts across diverse moral values, bypassing refusal behavior while preserving meaningful deliberative stakes.
AI.result:
1) Alignment faking is substantially more prevalent than previously reported, occurring in models as small as 7B parameters (olmo2-7b-instruct fakes alignment in 37% of cases).
2) Oversight conditions induce activation shifts along a single direction in representation space.
3) The behavioral divergence can be captured by a single contrastive steering vector, enabling lightweight inference-time mitigation with relative reductions in alignment faking of 85.8%, 94.0%, and 57.7% on different models.
AI.conclusion: Alignment faking is a widespread problem that can be effectively diagnosed using value-based conflict scenarios and mitigated using computationally efficient steering techniques based on representation space analysis.

title: FairyFuse: Multiplication-Free LLM Inference on CPUs via Fused Ternary Kernels
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed on CPU-only platforms where memory bandwidth is the primary bottleneck for autoregressive generation. Weight quantization to four bits or below reduces memory pressure, yet existing systems still dequantize weights and perform floating-point multiplications, limiting the achievable gains. Ternary weights in {-1, 0, +1} provide a more efficient alternative, replacing multiplications with conditional additions, subtractions, or no-ops. While Fairy2i shows that ternary LLMs can match FP16 quality, its runtime does not exploit this structure. We present FairyFuse, an inference system that enables multiplication-free execution on commodity CPUs by fusing the eight real-valued sub-GEMVs of each widely-linear layer into a single AVX-512 loop using masked additions and subtractions, with zero floating-point multiplications. Roofline analysis shows that 16x weight compression shifts memory-bound GEMV toward the compute regime on bandwidth-limited CPUs, yielding a 29.6x kernel speedup while offering little benefit on GPUs. End-to-end, FairyFuse achieves 32.4 tokens per second on a single Intel Xeon 8558P, outperforming llama.cpp Q4_K_M by 1.24x with near-lossless quality (WikiText-2 perplexity 5.52 vs. 5.47 FP16; downstream accuracy 66.0%).

AI.tldr: FairyFuse is a CPU inference system that enables multiplication-free execution of ternary LLMs using AVX-512 fused operations, achieving 1.24x speedup over llama.cpp Q4_K_M with near-lossless quality.
AI.motivation: Large language models deployed on CPU-only platforms face memory bandwidth bottlenecks for autoregressive generation. While weight quantization reduces memory pressure, existing systems still dequantize weights and perform floating-point multiplications, limiting performance gains. Ternary weights offer more efficient alternatives but existing runtimes don't exploit this structure.
AI.method: FairyFuse fuses eight real-valued sub-GEMVs of each widely-linear layer into a single AVX-512 loop using masked additions and subtractions, enabling multiplication-free execution on commodity CPUs. It leverages 16x weight compression to shift memory-bound GEMV operations toward the compute regime on bandwidth-limited CPUs.
AI.result: FairyFuse achieves 29.6x kernel speedup and 32.4 tokens per second on a single Intel Xeon 8558P, outperforming llama.cpp Q4_K_M by 1.24x. It maintains near-lossless quality with WikiText-2 perplexity of 5.52 vs. 5.47 FP16 and downstream accuracy of 66.0%.
AI.conclusion: Ternary weight quantization combined with multiplication-free execution on CPUs enables significant performance improvements for LLM inference on memory-bandwidth-limited platforms, while maintaining model quality comparable to FP16 baselines.

title: Learning to Communicate: Toward End-to-End Optimization of Multi-Agent Language Systems
summary: Multi-agent systems built on large language models have shown strong performance on complex reasoning tasks, yet most work focuses on agent roles and orchestration while treating inter-agent communication as a fixed interface. Latent communication through internal representations such as key-value caches offers a promising alternative to text-based protocols, but existing approaches do not jointly optimize communication with multi-agent reasoning. Therefore we propose DiffMAS, a training framework that treats latent communication as a learnable component of multi-agent systems. DiffMAS performs parameter-efficient supervised training over multi-agent latent trajectories, enabling agents to jointly learn how information should be encoded and interpreted across interactions. Experiments on mathematical reasoning, scientific QA, code generation, and commonsense benchmarks show that DiffMAS consistently improves reasoning accuracy and decoding stability over single-agent inference, text-based multi-agent systems, and prior latent communication methods, achieving 26.7% on AIME24, 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond, and consistent gains across reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: DiffMAS is a training framework that treats latent communication in multi-agent systems as learnable components, enabling agents to jointly optimize how information is encoded and interpreted across interactions for improved reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent systems focus on agent roles and orchestration while treating inter-agent communication as fixed interfaces. Existing approaches don't jointly optimize communication with multi-agent reasoning, limiting the potential of latent communication through internal representations like key-value caches.
AI.method: DiffMAS performs parameter-efficient supervised training over multi-agent latent trajectories, treating latent communication as a learnable component. The framework enables agents to jointly learn how information should be encoded and interpreted across interactions.
AI.result: Experiments show DiffMAS consistently improves reasoning accuracy and decoding stability over single-agent inference, text-based multi-agent systems, and prior latent communication methods, achieving 26.7% on AIME24, 20.2% on GPQA-Diamond, with consistent gains across reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Treating latent communication as a learnable component in multi-agent systems enables significant improvements in reasoning performance across diverse domains, demonstrating the importance of jointly optimizing communication protocols with multi-agent reasoning.

title: The Last Harness You'll Ever Build
summary: AI agents are increasingly deployed on complex, domain-specific workflows -- navigating enterprise web applications that require dozens of clicks and form fills, orchestrating multi-step research pipelines that span search, extraction, and synthesis, automating code review across unfamiliar repositories, and handling customer escalations that demand nuanced domain knowledge. \textbf{Each new task domain requires painstaking, expert-driven harness engineering}: designing the prompts, tools, orchestration logic, and evaluation criteria that make a foundation model effective. We present a two-level framework that automates this process. At the first level, the \textbf{Harness Evolution Loop} optimizes a worker agent's harness $\mathcal{H}$ for a single task: a Worker Agent $W_{\mathcal{H}}$ executes the task, an Evaluator Agent $V$ adversarially diagnoses failures and scores performance, and an Evolution Agent $E$ modifies the harness based on the full history of prior attempts. At the second level, the \textbf{Meta-Evolution Loop} optimizes the evolution protocol $Λ= (W_{\mathcal{H}}, \mathcal{H}^{(0)}, V, E)$ itself across diverse tasks, \textbf{learning a protocol $Λ^{(\text{best})}$ that enables rapid harness convergence on any new task -- so that adapting an agent to a novel domain requires no human harness engineering at all.} We formalize the correspondence to meta-learning and present both algorithms. The framework \textbf{shifts manual harness engineering into automated harness engineering}, and takes one step further -- \textbf{automating the design of the automation itself}.

AI.tldr: Two-level framework for automating AI agent harness engineering: Harness Evolution Loop optimizes single-task harnesses, and Meta-Evolution Loop learns evolution protocols that enable rapid adaptation to novel domains without human engineering.
AI.motivation: AI agents are deployed on complex domain-specific workflows that require extensive expert-driven harness engineering (prompts, tools, orchestration logic, evaluation criteria) for each new task domain. This manual engineering is painstaking and costly.
AI.method: Two-level framework: 1) Harness Evolution Loop with Worker Agent executing tasks, Evaluator Agent diagnosing failures/scoring performance, and Evolution Agent modifying harness based on history. 2) Meta-Evolution Loop that optimizes the evolution protocol itself across diverse tasks to learn protocols enabling rapid harness convergence on new tasks.
AI.result: The framework shifts manual harness engineering into automated harness engineering and further automates the design of automation itself, enabling adaptation to novel domains without human harness engineering.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach formalizes meta-learning correspondence and provides algorithms for automated harness engineering, eliminating the need for painstaking expert-driven engineering for each new AI agent deployment domain.

title: Absorber LLM: Harnessing Causal Synchronization for Test-Time Training
summary: Transformers suffer from a high computational cost that grows with sequence length for self-attention, making inference in long streams prohibited by memory consumption. Constant-memory alternatives such as RNNs and SSMs compress history into states with fixed size and thus lose long-tail dependencies, while methods that memorize contexts into parameters, such as Test-Time Training (TTT), are prone to overfitting token-level projection and fail to preserve the causal effect of context in pretrained LLMs. We propose Absorber LLM, which formulates long-context retention as a self-supervised causal synchronization: after absorbing historical contexts into parameters, a contextless model should match the original model with full context on future generations. We optimize this objective by synchronizing internal behaviors of the updated model with the original one, ensuring context absorption and generalization. Experiments on long-context and streaming benchmarks show that Absorber LLM reduces inference memory and improves accuracy over prior parameter-as-memory baselines.

AI.tldr: Absorber LLM is a novel approach that formulates long-context retention as self-supervised causal synchronization, enabling efficient long-stream inference by absorbing historical contexts into model parameters while preserving causal effects.
AI.motivation: Transformers suffer from high computational costs in long sequences due to self-attention memory consumption. Existing solutions like RNNs/SSMs lose long-tail dependencies when compressing history into fixed-size states, while Test-Time Training methods prone to overfitting and fail to preserve causal context effects in pretrained LLMs.
AI.method: The paper proposes Absorber LLM which formulates long-context retention as self-supervised causal synchronization. After absorbing historical contexts into parameters, the contextless model should match the original model with full context on future generations. The method optimizes this by synchronizing internal behaviors of the updated model with the original one, ensuring context absorption and generalization.
AI.result: Experiments on long-context and streaming benchmarks show that Absorber LLM reduces inference memory and improves accuracy over prior parameter-as-memory baselines.
AI.conclusion: Absorber LLM provides an effective solution for efficient long-context retention in LLMs, addressing both memory consumption and context preservation challenges through self-supervised causal synchronization.

title: Probably Approximately Consensus: On the Learning Theory of Finding Common Ground
comment: Accepted to the Social Choice and Learning Algorithms Workshop at IJCAI 2025
summary: A primary goal of online deliberation platforms is to identify ideas that are broadly agreeable to a community of users through their expressed preferences. Yet, consensus elicitation should ideally extend beyond the specific statements provided by users and should incorporate the relative salience of particular topics. We address this issue by modelling consensus as an interval in a one-dimensional opinion space derived from potentially high-dimensional data via embedding and dimensionality reduction. We define an objective that maximizes expected agreement within a hypothesis interval where the expectation is over an underlying distribution of issues, implicitly taking into account their salience. We propose an efficient Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) algorithm and establish PAC-learning guarantees. Our initial experiments demonstrate the performance of our algorithm and examine more efficient approaches to identifying optimal consensus regions. We find that through selectively querying users on an existing sample of statements, we can reduce the number of queries needed to a practical number.

AI.tldr: Researchers propose a new approach for online deliberation platforms that models consensus as intervals in an opinion space, maximizing expected agreement while accounting for topic salience.
AI.motivation: Current online deliberation platforms focus on identifying agreeable statements but fail to account for topic salience and don't generalize beyond specific user statements.
AI.method: Model consensus as intervals in a one-dimensional opinion space derived from embeddings, define objective maximizing expected agreement over issue distribution, propose ERM algorithm with PAC-learning guarantees, and experiment with selective querying to reduce required queries.
AI.result: Initial experiments demonstrate algorithm performance and show selective querying on existing statement samples can reduce needed queries to practical numbers.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework improves online deliberation by incorporating topic salience into consensus modeling while maintaining practical query efficiency through selective sampling strategies.

title: Deep FinResearch Bench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Conduct Professional Financial Investment Research
summary: We introduce Deep FinResearch Bench, a practical and comprehensive evaluation framework for deep research (DR) agents in financial investment research. The benchmark assesses three dimensions of report quality: qualitative rigor, quantitative forecasting and valuation accuracy, and claim credibility and verifiability. Particularly, we define corresponding qualitative and quantitative evaluation metrics and implement an automated scoring procedure to enable scalable assessment. Applying the benchmark to financial reports from frontier DR agents and comparing them with reports authored by financial professionals, we find that AI-generated reports still fall short across these dimensions. These findings underscore the need for domain-specialized DR agents tailored to finance, and we hope the work establishes a foundation for standardized benchmarking of DR agents in financial research.

AI.tldr: Deep FinResearch Bench: A comprehensive evaluation framework for deep research agents in financial investment research, assessing report quality across three dimensions with automated scoring. AI-generated reports still underperform compared to human financial professionals.
AI.motivation: There is a need for practical and comprehensive evaluation frameworks to assess deep research agents in financial investment research, as current AI-generated financial reports lack standardized benchmarking and systematic quality assessment.
AI.method: Developed Deep FinResearch Bench framework with three evaluation dimensions: qualitative rigor, quantitative forecasting/valuation accuracy, and claim credibility/verifiability. Defined corresponding qualitative and quantitative metrics and implemented automated scoring procedures for scalable assessment.
AI.result: When applied to financial reports from frontier deep research agents and compared with reports authored by financial professionals, AI-generated reports still fall short across all three quality dimensions (qualitative rigor, quantitative accuracy, and claim credibility).
AI.conclusion: AI-generated financial research reports currently underperform compared to human-authored reports, highlighting the need for domain-specialized deep research agents tailored to finance. This work establishes a foundation for standardized benchmarking of deep research agents in financial research.

title: The Path Not Taken: Duality in Reasoning about Program Execution
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities across diverse coding tasks. However, their adoption requires a true understanding of program execution rather than relying on surface-level patterns. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on predicting program properties tied to specific inputs (e.g., code coverage, program outputs). As a result, they provide a narrow view of dynamic code reasoning and are prone to data contamination. We argue that understanding program execution requires evaluating its inherent duality through two complementary reasoning tasks: (i) predicting a program's observed behavior for a given input, and (ii) inferring how the input must be mutated toward a specific behavioral objective. Both tasks jointly probe a model's causal understanding of execution flow. We instantiate this duality in DexBench, a benchmark comprising 445 paired instances, and evaluate 13 LLMs. Our results demonstrate that dual-path reasoning provides a robust and discriminative proxy for dynamic code understanding.

AI.tldr: DexBench introduces a new benchmark for evaluating LLMs' dynamic code reasoning through dual-path reasoning: predicting program behavior for given inputs and inferring input mutations for specific behavioral objectives.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM code understanding are limited - they focus on predicting program properties tied to specific inputs (like code coverage or outputs), providing only a narrow view of dynamic code reasoning and being prone to data contamination. The authors argue that true understanding of program execution requires evaluating both forward (predicting behavior) and backward (inferring input mutations) reasoning.
AI.method: The authors propose dual-path reasoning as the foundation for evaluating dynamic code understanding: (1) predicting a program's observed behavior for a given input, and (2) inferring how the input must be mutated toward a specific behavioral objective. They instantiate this approach in DexBench, a benchmark comprising 445 paired instances that evaluate both reasoning tasks.
AI.result: The researchers evaluated 13 LLMs on DexBench. Their results demonstrate that dual-path reasoning provides a robust and discriminative proxy for dynamic code understanding, offering better assessment of models' causal understanding of execution flow compared to existing benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DexBench's dual-path reasoning approach offers a more comprehensive framework for evaluating LLMs' dynamic code understanding. By requiring models to demonstrate both forward and backward reasoning capabilities, it provides better insight into whether models truly understand program execution rather than just surface-level patterns.

title: Adaptive Test-Time Compute Allocation with Evolving In-Context Demonstrations
summary: While scaling test-time compute can substantially improve model performance, existing approaches either rely on static compute allocation or sample from fixed generation distributions. In this work, we introduce a test-time compute allocation framework that jointly adapts where computation is spent and how generation is performed. Our method begins with a warm-up phase that identifies easy queries and assembles an initial pool of question-response pairs from the test set itself. An adaptive phase then concentrates further computation on unresolved queries while reshaping their generation distributions through evolving in-context demonstrations -- conditioning each generation on successful responses from semantically related queries rather than resampling from a fixed distribution. Experiments across math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms existing baselines while consuming substantially less inference-time compute.

AI.tldr: A dynamic test-time compute allocation framework that adaptively focuses computation on hard queries while using successful responses from similar queries as in-context examples, reducing inference compute while improving performance.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for test-time compute scaling use static compute allocation or fixed generation distributions, lacking adaptivity. The paper aims to develop a more efficient method that dynamically allocates computation based on query difficulty and improves generation quality through adaptive demonstration selection.
AI.method: Two-phase approach: 1) Warm-up phase identifies easy queries and builds an initial pool of question-response pairs from the test set itself. 2) Adaptive phase concentrates computation on unresolved queries while reshaping their generation distributions through evolving in-context demonstrations - conditioning each generation on successful responses from semantically related queries rather than fixed distributions.
AI.result: Experiments across math, coding, and reasoning benchmarks show the approach consistently outperforms existing baselines while consuming substantially less inference-time compute.
AI.conclusion: The proposed adaptive test-time compute allocation framework provides an effective way to improve model performance with reduced computational cost by dynamically focusing computation on difficult queries and leveraging successful solutions from related problems as context.

title: Forget, Then Recall: Learnable Compression and Selective Unfolding via Gist Sparse Attention
summary: Scaling large language models to long contexts is challenging due to the quadratic computational cost of full attention. Mitigation approaches include KV-cache selection or compression techniques. We instead provide an effective and end-to-end learnable bridge between the two without requiring architecture modification. In particular, our key insight is that interleaved gist compression tokens -- which provide a learnable summary of sets of raw tokens -- can serve as routing signals for sparse attention. Building on this, we introduce selective unfolding via GSA, which first compresses the context into gist tokens, then selects the most relevant gists, and subsequently restores the corresponding raw chunks for detailed attention. This yields a simple coarse-to-fine mechanism that combines compact global representations with targeted access to fine-grained evidence. We further incorporate this process directly into training in an end-to-end fashion, avoiding the need for external retrieval modules. In addition, we extend the framework hierarchically via recursive gist-of-gist construction, enabling multi-resolution context access with logarithmic per-step decoding complexity. Empirical results on LongBench and RAG benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms other compression baselines as well as inference-time sparse attention methods across compression ratios from $8\times$ to $32\times$. The code is available at: https://github.com/yuzhenmao/gist-sparse-attention/

AI.tldr: Gist-sparse attention: An end-to-end learnable method that uses interleaved gist compression tokens to enable efficient coarse-to-fine attention for long contexts without architecture changes.
AI.motivation: Scaling LLMs to long contexts is challenging due to quadratic computational cost of full attention. Existing approaches like KV-cache selection or compression techniques have limitations, so the authors seek a more effective approach that doesn't require architecture modifications.
AI.method: Introduces selective unfolding via GSA: 1) compresses context into gist tokens (learnable summaries), 2) selects most relevant gists, 3) restores corresponding raw chunks for detailed attention. Uses end-to-end training without external retrieval modules, and extends hierarchically via recursive gist-of-gist construction for multi-resolution access.
AI.result: Method consistently outperforms other compression baselines and inference-time sparse attention methods across compression ratios from 8× to 32× on LongBench and RAG benchmarks. Achieves logarithmic per-step decoding complexity.
AI.conclusion: Gist compression tokens can effectively serve as routing signals for sparse attention, creating a practical coarse-to-fine mechanism that combines global representations with targeted access to fine-grained evidence for efficient long-context processing.

title: HypEHR: Hyperbolic Modeling of Electronic Health Records for Efficient Question Answering
summary: Electronic health record (EHR) question answering is often handled by LLM-based pipelines that are costly to deploy and do not explicitly leverage the hierarchical structure of clinical data. Motivated by evidence that medical ontologies and patient trajectories exhibit hyperbolic geometry, we propose HypEHR, a compact Lorentzian model that embeds codes, visits, and questions in hyperbolic space and answers queries via geometry-consistent cross-attention with type-specific pointer heads. HypEHR is pretrained with next-visit diagnosis prediction and hierarchy-aware regularization to align representations with the ICD ontology. On two MIMIC-IV-based EHR-QA benchmarks, HypEHR approaches LLM-based methods while using far fewer parameters. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yuyuliu11037/HypEHR.

AI.tldr: HypEHR: A compact hyperbolic model for EHR question answering that uses fewer parameters than LLM-based methods while achieving comparable performance by leveraging hyperbolic geometry and medical ontology structure.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based EHR question answering pipelines are costly to deploy and fail to explicitly leverage the hierarchical structure of clinical data. Medical ontologies and patient trajectories exhibit hyperbolic geometry, suggesting a more efficient approach.
AI.method: HypEHR uses Lorentzian hyperbolic space embeddings for medical codes, visits, and questions. It employs geometry-consistent cross-attention with type-specific pointer heads, pretrained on next-visit diagnosis prediction with hierarchy-aware regularization aligned with ICD ontology structure.
AI.result: On two MIMIC-IV-based EHR-QA benchmarks, HypEHR approaches the performance of LLM-based methods while using far fewer parameters, demonstrating efficient and effective EHR question answering.
AI.conclusion: HypEHR presents an efficient alternative to costly LLM-based EHR QA systems by leveraging hyperbolic geometry and medical ontology structure, offering comparable performance with substantially reduced computational requirements.

title: Validating a Deep Learning Algorithm to Identify Patients with Glaucoma using Systemic Electronic Health Records
summary: We evaluated whether a glaucoma risk assessment (GRA) model trained on All of Us national data can identify patients at high probability of glaucoma using only systemic electronic health records (EHR) at an independent institution. In this cross-sectional study, 20,636 Stanford patients seen from November 2013 to January 2024 were included (15% with glaucoma). A pretrained GRA model was fine-tuned on the Stanford cohort and tested on a held-out set using demographics, systemic diagnoses, medications, laboratory results, and physical examination measurements as inputs. The best model achieved AUROC 0.883 and PPV 0.657. Calibration was consistent with clinical risk: the highest prediction decile showed the greatest glaucoma diagnosis rate (65.7%) and treatment rate (57.0%). Performance improved with more trainable layers up to 15 and with additional data. An EHR-only GRA model may enable scalable and accessible pre-screening without specialized imaging.

AI.tldr: A glaucoma risk assessment model trained on national data can effectively identify high-risk patients using only systemic EHR data, achieving AUROC of 0.883 and good clinical calibration.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to develop a scalable glaucoma screening method that doesn't require specialized imaging equipment, making pre-screening more accessible using only routinely available electronic health records data.
AI.method: Cross-sectional study of 20,636 Stanford patients (2013-2024) with 15% glaucoma prevalence. A pretrained GRA model was fine-tuned on the Stanford cohort using demographics, systemic diagnoses, medications, lab results, and physical exam measurements. Model was tested on held-out set.
AI.result: Best model achieved AUROC of 0.883 and PPV of 0.657. Calibration aligned with clinical risk - highest prediction decile had 65.7% glaucoma diagnosis rate and 57.0% treatment rate. Performance improved with more trainable layers (up to 15) and with additional data.
AI.conclusion: An EHR-only glaucoma risk assessment model can enable scalable, accessible pre-screening without specialized imaging, potentially improving early detection and clinical workflow efficiency.

title: Who Defines Fairness? Target-Based Prompting for Demographic Representation in Generative Models
summary: Text-to-image(T2I) models like Stable Diffusion and DALL-E have made generative AI widely accessible, yet recent studies reveal that these systems often replicate societal biases, particularly in how they depict demographic groups across professions. Prompts such as 'doctor' or 'CEO' frequently yield lighter-skinned outputs, while lower-status roles like 'janitor' show more diversity, reinforcing stereotypes. Existing mitigation methods typically require retraining or curated datasets, making them inaccessible to most users. We propose a lightweight, inference-time framework that mitigates representational bias through prompt-level intervention without modifying the underlying model. Instead of assuming a single definition of fairness, our approach allows users to select among multiple fairness specifications-ranging from simple choices such as a uniform distribution to more complex definitions informed by a large language model(LLM) that cites sources and provides confidence estimates. These distributions guide the construction of demographic specific prompt variants in the corresponding proportions, and we evaluate alignment by auditing adherence to the declared target and measuring the resulting skin tone distribution rather than assuming uniformity as 'fairness'. Across 36 prompts spanning 30 occupations and 6 non-occupational contexts, our method shifts observed skin-tone outcomes in directions consistent with the declared target, and reduces deviation from targets when the target is defined directly in skin-tone space(fallback). This work demonstrates how fairness interventions can be made transparent, controllable, and usable at inference time, directly empowering users of generative AI.

AI.tldr: A lightweight inference-time framework that mitigates representational bias in text-to-image models through user-selectable fairness specifications and prompt interventions, without retraining the model.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image models like Stable Diffusion and DALL-E exhibit societal biases by disproportionately generating lighter-skinned outputs for high-status professions and more diverse outputs for lower-status roles, reinforcing harmful stereotypes. Existing bias mitigation methods require retraining or curated datasets, making them inaccessible to most users.
AI.method: A lightweight inference-time framework using prompt-level interventions without model modifications. Users can select from multiple fairness specifications (from simple uniform distributions to complex LLM-informed ones with citations and confidence estimates). These distributions guide construction of demographic-specific prompt variants in target proportions, with alignment audited by measuring adherence to declared targets in skin-tone space.
AI.result: The method successfully shifts observed skin-tone distributions across 36 prompts (30 occupations + 6 non-occupational contexts) to align with declared fairness targets. It reduces deviation from targets when targets are defined directly in skin-tone space (fallback). Demonstrates practical bias reduction at inference time.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates that fairness interventions can be made transparent, controllable, and usable at inference time, directly empowering generative AI users with accessible bias mitigation without requiring model retraining or complex technical expertise.

title: ILDR: Geometric Early Detection of Grokking
summary: Grokking describes a delayed generalization phenomenon in which a neural network achieves perfect training accuracy long before validation accuracy improves, followed by an abrupt transition to strong generalization. Existing detection signals are indirect: weight norm reflects parameter-space regularization and consistently lags the transition, while GrokFast's slow gradient EMA, used without gradient amplification, is unstable across seeds with standard deviation exceeding mean lead time. We propose the Inter/Intra-class Distance Ratio (ILDR), a geometric metric computed on second-to-last layer representations as the ratio of inter-class centroid separation to intra-class scatter. ILDR provides an early detection signal: it rises and crosses a threshold at 2.5 times its baseline before the grokking transition appears in validation accuracy, indicating early geometric reorganization in representation space. Grounded in Fisher's linear discriminant criterion, ILDR requires no eigendecomposition and runs in O(|C|^2 + N). It is evaluated exclusively on held-out data, making it robust to memorization effects. Across modular arithmetic and permutation group composition (S5), ILDR leads the grokking transition by 9 to 73 percent of the training budget, with lead time increasing with task algebraic complexity. Over eight random seeds, ILDR leads by 950 +/- 250 steps with a coefficient of variation of 26 percent, and post-grokking variance drops by 1696 times, consistent with a sharp phase transition in representation space. Using ILDR as an early stopping trigger reduces training by 18.6 percent on average. Optimizer interventions triggered at the ILDR threshold demonstrate bidirectional control over the transition, suggesting ILDR tracks representational conditions underlying generalization rather than a downstream correlate.

AI.tldr: A novel geometric metric called Inter/Intra-class Distance Ratio (ILDR) provides early detection of grokking transitions in neural networks by measuring representation space reorganization before validation accuracy improves.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for detecting grokking transitions are problematic: weight norm is an indirect signal that lags behind the transition, while GrokFast's gradient-based approach is unstable across random seeds and suffers from high variance.
AI.method: The authors propose ILDR, computed on second-to-last layer representations as the ratio of inter-class centroid separation to intra-class scatter. This metric is grounded in Fisher's linear discriminant criterion, requires no eigendecomposition, runs efficiently, and is evaluated exclusively on held-out data to avoid memorization effects.
AI.result: ILDR provides early detection 2.5 times above baseline before the grokking transition appears in validation accuracy. It leads the transition by 9-73% of training budget across tasks, with lead time increasing with algebraic complexity. Using ILDR for early stopping reduces training by 18.6% on average, and optimizer interventions at the ILDR threshold demonstrate bidirectional control over the transition.
AI.conclusion: ILDR is a robust early detection signal for grokking that tracks representational conditions underlying generalization rather than being a downstream correlate. It enables early stopping and intervention, making it a practical tool for training neural networks that undergo delayed generalization.

title: Active Data
summary: In some complex domains, certain problem-specific decompositions can provide advantages over monolithic designs by enabling comprehension and specification of the design. In this paper we present an intuitive and tractable approach to reasoning over large and complex data sets. Our approach is based on Active Data, i.e., data as atomic objects that actively interact with environments. We describe our intuition about how this bottom-up approach improves designs confronting computational and conceptual complexity. We describe an implementation of the base Active Data concepts within the air traffic flow management domain and discuss performance for this implementation.

AI.tldr: A bottom-up approach using Active Data (data as atomic interactive objects) improves handling of complex domains by enabling decomposition and better reasoning over large datasets.
AI.motivation: In complex domains, monolithic designs struggle with computational and conceptual complexity. Problem-specific decompositions can provide advantages by enabling better comprehension and specification of designs, but current approaches lack intuitive ways to reason over large and complex datasets.
AI.method: The paper introduces Active Data - an approach where data is treated as atomic objects that actively interact with environments. This bottom-up approach provides a tractable framework for decomposition and reasoning. The implementation is demonstrated within the air traffic flow management domain.
AI.result: The paper describes the implementation of Active Data concepts in air traffic flow management and discusses performance results, though specific metrics are not detailed in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Active Data provides an intuitive and tractable bottom-up approach that improves design capabilities when confronting computational and conceptual complexity in complex domains, particularly for reasoning over large datasets.

title: Clinically Interpretable Sepsis Early Warning via LLM-Guided Simulation of Temporal Physiological Dynamics
summary: Timely and interpretable early warning of sepsis remains a major clinical challenge due to the complex temporal dynamics of physiological deterioration. Traditional data-driven models often provide accurate yet opaque predictions, limiting physicians' confidence and clinical applicability. To address this limitation, we propose a Large Language Model (LLM)-guided temporal simulation framework that explicitly models physiological trajectories prior to disease onset for clinically interpretable prediction. The framework consists of a spatiotemporal feature extraction module that captures dynamic dependencies among multivariate vital signs, a Medical Prompt-as-Prefix module that embeds clinical reasoning cues into LLMs, and an agent-based post-processing component that constrains predictions within physiologically plausible ranges. By first simulating the evolution of key physiological indicators and then classifying sepsis onset, our model offers transparent prediction mechanisms that align with clinical judgment. Evaluated on the MIMIC-IV and eICU databases, the proposed method achieves superior AUC scores (0.861-0.903) across 24-4-hour pre-onset prediction tasks, outperforming conventional deep learning and rule-based approaches. More importantly, it provides interpretable trajectories and risk trends that can assist clinicians in early intervention and personalized decision-making in intensive care environments.

AI.tldr: LLM-guided temporal simulation framework for sepsis prediction that generates interpretable physiological trajectories to provide transparent early warnings in ICU settings.
AI.motivation: Timely and interpretable sepsis early warning is challenging due to complex physiological dynamics. Traditional models provide accurate but opaque predictions, limiting clinical confidence and applicability.
AI.method: Three-component framework: 1) Spatiotemporal feature extraction module for dynamic dependencies in vital signs, 2) Medical Prompt-as-Prefix module embedding clinical reasoning cues into LLMs, 3) Agent-based post-processing to constrain predictions within physiologically plausible ranges. Simulates physiological evolution first, then classifies sepsis onset.
