This paper introduces Deductive ASPIC$^{\ominus}$, a novel framework that integrates gen-rebuttals from ASPIC$^{\ominus}$ with the Joint Support Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (JSBAFs) of Deductive ASPIC$-$, incorporating preferences. We show that Deductive ASPIC$^{\ominus}$ satisfies all five rationality postulates under a version of preferred semantics. This work opens new avenues for further research on robust and logically sound structured argumentation systems.

AI.tldr: Deductive ASPIC⊖ is a new structured argumentation framework that satisfies all five key rationality postulates under preferred semantics by integrating gen-rebuttals from ASPIC⊖ with JSBAFs from Deductive ASPIC-.
AI.motivation: Existing ASPIC-style frameworks like ASPIC⊖ and Deductive ASPIC- fail to satisfy all five critical rationality postulates (closure, direct/indirect consistency, non-interference, crash-resistance) simultaneously under credulous semantics when undercuts are present, creating a gap in robust logical argumentation.
AI.method: The paper introduces Deductive ASPIC⊖, which combines gen-rebuttals from ASPIC⊖ with Joint Support Bipolar Argumentation Frameworks (JSBAFs) from Deductive ASPIC-, incorporating preferences into the framework.
AI.result: Deductive ASPIC⊖ is shown to satisfy all five rationality postulates under a version of preferred semantics, overcoming the limitations of previous approaches.
AI.conclusion: This work successfully addresses the gap in satisfying all rationality postulates simultaneously and opens new research directions for robust and logically sound structured argumentation systems.

title: CAP: Controllable Alignment Prompting for Unlearning in LLMs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) trained on unfiltered corpora inherently risk retaining sensitive information, necessitating selective knowledge unlearning for regulatory compliance and ethical safety. However, existing parameter-modifying methods face fundamental limitations: high computational costs, uncontrollable forgetting boundaries, and strict dependency on model weight access. These constraints render them impractical for closed-source models, yet current non-invasive alternatives remain unsystematic and reliant on empirical experience. To address these challenges, we propose the Controllable Alignment Prompting for Unlearning (CAP) framework, an end-to-end prompt-driven unlearning paradigm. CAP decouples unlearning into a learnable prompt optimization process via reinforcement learning, where a prompt generator collaborates with the LLM to suppress target knowledge while preserving general capabilities selectively. This approach enables reversible knowledge restoration through prompt revocation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CAP achieves precise, controllable unlearning without updating model parameters, establishing a dynamic alignment mechanism that overcomes the transferability limitations of prior methods.

AI.tldr: CAP is a prompt-driven framework that enables precise, controllable knowledge unlearning in LLMs without modifying model parameters, using reinforcement learning to optimize prompts that suppress specific knowledge while preserving general capabilities.
AI.motivation: LLMs trained on unfiltered data risk retaining sensitive information, requiring selective knowledge unlearning for regulatory compliance and ethical safety. Existing parameter-modifying methods have high computational costs, uncontrollable forgetting boundaries, and require model weight access, making them impractical for closed-source models.
AI.method: CAP framework uses an end-to-end prompt-driven unlearning paradigm that decouples unlearning into a learnable prompt optimization process via reinforcement learning. A prompt generator collaborates with the LLM to suppress target knowledge while preserving general capabilities selectively.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that CAP achieves precise, controllable unlearning without updating model parameters, establishing a dynamic alignment mechanism that overcomes the transferability limitations of prior methods. The approach enables reversible knowledge restoration through prompt revocation.
AI.conclusion: CAP provides a practical, non-invasive solution for knowledge unlearning that works on closed-source models, offering controllable forgetting with the ability to restore knowledge when needed, addressing both regulatory compliance and ethical safety concerns in LLMs.

title: The CriticalSet problem: Identifying Critical Contributors in Bipartite Dependency Networks
summary: Identifying critical nodes in complex networks is a fundamental task in graph mining. Yet, methods addressing an all-or-nothing coverage mechanics in a bipartite dependency network, a graph with two types of nodes where edges represent dependency relationships across the two groups only, remain largely unexplored. We formalize the CriticalSet problem: given an arbitrary bipartite graph modeling dependencies of items on contributors, identify the set of k contributors whose removal isolates the largest number of items. We prove that this problem is NP-hard and requires maximizing a supermodular set function, for which standard forward greedy algorithms provide no approximation guarantees. Consequently, we model CriticalSet as a coalitional game, deriving a closed-form centrality, ShapleyCov, based on the Shapley value. This measure can be interpreted as the expected number of items isolated by a contributor's departure. Leveraging these insights, we propose MinCov, a linear-time iterative peeling algorithm that explicitly accounts for connection redundancy, prioritizing contributors who uniquely support many items. Extensive experiments on synthetic and large-scale real datasets, including a Wikipedia graph with over 250 million edges, reveal that MinCov and ShapleyCov significantly outperform traditional baselines. Notably, MinCov achieves near-optimal performance, within 0.02 AUC of a Stochastic Hill Climbing metaheuristic, while remaining several orders of magnitude faster.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes the CriticalSet problem for bipartite dependency networks, proves it's NP-hard, develops ShapleyCov centrality measure based on Shapley value, and proposes MinCov algorithm that outperforms traditional baselines while being highly efficient.
AI.motivation: Critical node identification is fundamental in graph mining, but methods for bipartite dependency networks with all-or-nothing coverage mechanics (where edges represent dependencies across two node groups) remain largely unexplored. There's a need to identify critical contributors in such networks.
AI.method: The paper formalizes CriticalSet problem for bipartite dependency graphs, proves NP-hardness, models it as a coalitional game to derive ShapleyCov centrality measure (Shapley value-based), and proposes MinCov algorithm - a linear-time iterative peeling algorithm that accounts for connection redundancy by prioritizing contributors uniquely supporting many items.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on synthetic and real datasets (including Wikipedia with 250M+ edges) show MinCov and ShapleyCov significantly outperform traditional baselines. MinCov achieves near-optimal performance (within 0.02 AUC of Stochastic Hill Climbing metaheuristic) while being orders of magnitude faster.
AI.conclusion: The CriticalSet problem is NP-hard and requires novel approaches. The proposed ShapleyCov centrality and MinCov algorithm provide effective solutions for identifying critical contributors in bipartite dependency networks, with MinCov offering excellent performance-efficiency trade-off.

title: Improving Performance in Classification Tasks with LCEN and the Weighted Focal Differentiable MCC Loss
summary: The LASSO-Clip-EN (LCEN) algorithm was previously introduced for nonlinear, interpretable feature selection and machine learning. However, its design and use was limited to regression tasks. In this work, we create a modified version of the LCEN algorithm that is suitable for classification tasks and maintains its desirable properties, such as interpretability. This modified LCEN algorithm is evaluated on four widely used binary and multiclass classification datasets. In these experiments, LCEN is compared against 10 other model types and consistently reaches high test-set macro F$_1$ score and Matthews correlation coefficient (MCC) metrics, higher than that of the majority of investigated models. LCEN models for classification remain sparse, eliminating an average of 56% of all input features in the experiments performed. Furthermore, LCEN-selected features are used to retrain all models using the same data, leading to statistically significant performance improvements in three of the experiments and insignificant differences in the fourth when compared to using all features or other feature selection methods. Simultaneously, the weighted focal differentiable MCC (diffMCC) loss function is evaluated on the same datasets. Models trained with the diffMCC loss function are always the best-performing methods in these experiments, and reach test-set macro F$_1$ scores that are, on average, 4.9% higher and MCCs that are 8.5% higher than those obtained by models trained with the weighted cross-entropy loss. These results highlight the performance of LCEN as a feature selection and machine learning algorithm also for classification tasks, and how the diffMCC loss function can train very accurate models, surpassing the weighted cross-entropy loss in the tasks investigated.

AI.tldr: LCEN algorithm extended from regression to classification, maintaining interpretability and sparsity. New diffMCC loss function outperforms cross-entropy. Both achieve high performance on classification tasks with effective feature selection.
AI.motivation: The original LCEN algorithm was limited to regression tasks. This work aims to extend LCEN to classification while preserving its desirable properties like interpretability and sparsity, and to evaluate a new differentiable MCC loss function for better classification performance.
AI.method: Modified LCEN algorithm for classification tasks and introduced weighted focal differentiable MCC (diffMCC) loss function. Evaluated on four binary and multiclass classification datasets, comparing against 10 other model types. Used LCEN-selected features to retrain all models.
AI.result: LCEN achieved high test-set macro F1 scores and MCC metrics, outperforming most other models. Eliminated average 56% of input features. LCEN feature selection led to statistically significant performance improvements in 3 of 4 experiments. Models trained with diffMCC loss consistently outperformed weighted cross-entropy, with 4.9% higher F1 scores and 8.5% higher MCCs on average.
AI.conclusion: LCEN is effective for feature selection and machine learning in classification tasks, maintaining interpretability and sparsity. The diffMCC loss function trains more accurate models than weighted cross-entropy for the investigated classification problems.

title: Unbiased Prevalence Estimation with Multicalibrated LLMs
summary: Estimating the prevalence of a category in a population using imperfect measurement devices (diagnostic tests, classifiers, or large language models) is fundamental to science, public health, and online trust and safety. Standard approaches correct for known device error rates but assume these rates remain stable across populations. We show this assumption fails under covariate shift and that multicalibration, which enforces calibration conditional on the input features rather than just on average, is sufficient for unbiased prevalence estimation under such shift. Standard calibration and quantification methods fail to provide this guarantee. Our work connects recent theoretical work on fairness to a longstanding measurement problem spanning nearly all academic disciplines. A simulation confirms that standard methods exhibit bias growing with shift magnitude, while a multicalibrated estimator maintains near-zero bias. While we focus the discussion mostly on LLMs, our theoretical results apply to any classification model. Two empirical applications -- estimating employment prevalence across U.S. states using the American Community Survey, and classifying political texts across four countries using an LLM -- demonstrate that multicalibration substantially reduces bias in practice, while highlighting that calibration data should cover the key feature dimensions along which target populations may differ.

AI.tldr: Multicalibration (calibration conditional on input features) enables unbiased prevalence estimation under covariate shift, unlike standard calibration methods which fail when error rates change across populations.
AI.motivation: Standard prevalence estimation methods using imperfect measurement devices assume stable error rates across populations, but this assumption fails under covariate shift where device performance varies across different populations or contexts.
AI.method: The authors propose using multicalibration, which enforces calibration conditional on input features rather than just average calibration, to achieve unbiased prevalence estimation under covariate shift. They provide theoretical analysis and validate through simulations and empirical applications.
AI.result: Standard calibration and quantification methods show increasing bias with shift magnitude, while multicalibrated estimators maintain near-zero bias. Empirical applications with employment prevalence estimation and political text classification demonstrate substantial bias reduction in practice.
AI.conclusion: Multicalibration provides robust prevalence estimation under covariate shift, connecting fairness theory to measurement problems across disciplines. Calibration data must cover key feature dimensions where target populations may differ to ensure accurate estimation.

title: Hyperloop Transformers
summary: LLM architecture research generally aims to maximize model quality subject to fixed compute/latency budgets. However, many applications of interest such as edge and on-device deployment are further constrained by the model's memory footprint, thus motivating parameter-efficient architectures for language modeling. This paper describes a simple architecture that improves the parameter-efficiency of LLMs. Our architecture makes use of looped Transformers as a core primitive, which reuse Transformer layers across depth and are thus more parameter-efficient than ordinary (depth-matched) Transformers. We organize the looped Transformer into three blocks--begin, middle, and end blocks--where each block itself consists of multiple Transformer layers, and only the middle block is applied recurrently across depth. We augment the looped middle block with hyper-connections (Xie et al., 2026), which expand the residual stream into matrix-valued residual streams. Hyper-connections are applied only after each loop, and therefore add minimal new parameters and compute cost. Across various model scales, we find that our Hyper-Connected Looped Transformer (Hyperloop Transformer) is able to outperform depth-matched Transformer and mHC Transformer baselines despite using approximately 50% fewer parameters. The outperformance persists through post-training weight quantization, thus positioning Hyperloop Transformers as an attractive architecture for memory-efficient language modeling.

AI.tldr: Hyper-Connected Looped Transformer (Hyperloop Transformer) improves parameter efficiency by using looped Transformers with hyper-connections, achieving better performance with 50% fewer parameters than traditional Transformers.
AI.motivation: Many applications like edge and on-device deployment have strict memory constraints, requiring parameter-efficient LLM architectures that maintain quality while reducing memory footprint.
AI.method: Uses looped Transformers that reuse layers across depth, organized into begin, middle, and end blocks. Only the middle block is applied recurrently, augmented with hyper-connections (matrix-valued residual streams) applied after each loop to minimize parameter/compute overhead.
AI.result: Hyperloop Transformers outperform depth-matched Transformers and mHC Transformer baselines across various scales despite using ~50% fewer parameters. Performance advantage persists through post-training weight quantization.
AI.conclusion: Hyperloop Transformers provide an attractive architecture for memory-efficient language modeling, offering better performance with significantly fewer parameters while maintaining compatibility with quantization techniques.

title: Engaged AI Governance: Addressing the Last Mile Challenge Through Internal Expert Collaboration
summary: Under the EU AI Act, translating AI governance requirements into software development practice remains challenging. While AI governance frameworks exist at industry and organizational levels, empirical evidence of team-level implementation is scarce. We address this "Last Mile" Challenge through insider action research embedded within an AI startup. We present a legal-text-to-action pipeline that translates EU AI Act requirements into actionable strategies through internal expert collaboration by extracting requirements from legal text, engaging practitioners in assessment and ideation, and prioritizing implementation through collective evaluation. Our analysis reveals three patterns in how practitioners perceive regulatory requirements: convergence (compliance aligns with development priorities), existing practice (current work already satisfies requirements), and disconnection (requirements perceived as administrative overhead). Based on these patterns, we discuss when governance might be treated genuinely or performatively. Practitioners prioritize requirements that serve end-users or their own development needs, but view verification-oriented requirements as box-ticking exercises. This distinction suggests a translation challenge: regulatory requirements risk superficial treatment unless practitioners understand how compliance serves system quality and user protection. Expert collaboration offers a practical mechanism for transforming governance from external imposition to shared ownership and making previously invisible governance work visible and collective.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the "Last Mile" challenge of implementing EU AI Act requirements in AI startups through a legal-text-to-action pipeline, revealing three patterns in practitioner perceptions of regulation and discussing genuine vs. performative governance implementation.
AI.motivation: There's a significant gap between AI governance requirements (particularly under the EU AI Act) and actual implementation at the team level in software development practice. While governance frameworks exist at higher levels, empirical evidence of practical, team-level implementation is scarce, creating a "Last Mile" challenge.
AI.method: The researchers conducted insider action research embedded within an AI startup, developing a legal-text-to-action pipeline that: 1) extracts requirements from legal text, 2) engages practitioners in assessment and ideation, and 3) prioritizes implementation through collective evaluation. This approach uses internal expert collaboration to translate legal requirements into actionable strategies.
AI.result: The research identified three patterns in how practitioners perceive regulatory requirements: convergence (compliance aligns with development priorities), existing practice (current work already satisfies requirements), and disconnection (requirements seen as administrative overhead). Practitioners prioritize requirements that benefit end-users or their own development needs but view verification-oriented requirements as mere box-ticking exercises.
AI.conclusion: Expert collaboration offers a practical mechanism to transform governance from external imposition to shared ownership, making governance work visible and collective. The key translation challenge is that regulatory requirements risk superficial treatment unless practitioners understand how compliance serves system quality and user protection. This distinction between genuine and performative governance implementation is crucial for effective regulatory compliance.

title: Measure Twice, Click Once: Co-evolving Proposer and Visual Critic via Reinforcement Learning for GUI Grounding
summary: Graphical User Interface (GUI) grounding requires mapping natural language instructions to precise pixel coordinates. However, due to visually homogeneous elements and dense layouts, models typically grasp semantic intent yet struggle with achieving precise localization. While scaling sampling attempts (Pass@k) reveals potential gains, static self-consistency strategies derived from geometric clustering often yield limited improvements, as the model's predictions tend to be spatially dispersed. In this paper, we propose replacing static consistency strategies with a learnable selection mechanism that selects the optimal target by critiquing its own proposals rendered on the screenshot. Given the significant disparity between the model's grounding and critiquing capabilities, we propose a co-evolving Propose-then-Critic framework. To jointly optimize these, we introduce a maturity-aware adaptive co-evolutionary reinforcement learning paradigm. This approach dynamically balances the training objectives of proposer and critic, where the diversity of the proposer's outputs enhances critic robustness, while the critic's maturing discrimination capability conversely unlocks the proposer's potential for extensive spatial exploration, fostering the mutual reinforcement and co-evolution of both capabilities, thereby ensuring generalizability to adapt to diverse and complex interface layouts. Extensive experiments over 6 benchmarks show that our method significantly enhances both grounding accuracy and critic reliability.

AI.tldr: Propose-then-Critic framework with co-evolving RL for GUI grounding replaces static consistency strategies with learnable selection, improving both localization accuracy and critic reliability.
AI.motivation: Current GUI grounding models grasp semantic intent but struggle with precise pixel localization due to visually homogeneous elements and dense layouts. Static self-consistency strategies (geometric clustering) yield limited improvements as predictions tend to be spatially dispersed.
AI.method: Propose-then-Critic framework with learnable selection mechanism that critiques proposals rendered on screenshots. Uses maturity-aware adaptive co-evolutionary reinforcement learning to jointly optimize proposer and critic capabilities, balancing their training objectives dynamically.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 6 benchmarks show significant improvements in both grounding accuracy and critic reliability compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The co-evolving framework enables mutual reinforcement between proposer's spatial exploration and critic's discrimination capability, ensuring better adaptation to diverse and complex interface layouts for GUI grounding tasks.

title: Probabilistic Verification of Neural Networks via Efficient Probabilistic Hull Generation
summary: The problem of probabilistic verification of a neural network investigates the probability of satisfying the safe constraints in the output space when the input is given by a probability distribution. It is significant to answer this problem when the input is affected by disturbances often modeled by probabilistic variables. In the paper, we propose a novel neural network probabilistic verification framework which computes a guaranteed range for the safe probability by efficiently finding safe and unsafe probabilistic hulls. Our approach consists of three main innovations: (1) a state space subdivision strategy using regression trees to produce probabilistic hulls, (2) a boundary-aware sampling method which identifies the safety boundary in the input space using samples that are later used for building regression trees, and (3) iterative refinement with probabilistic prioritization for computing a guaranteed range for the safe probability. The accuracy and efficiency of our approach are evaluated on various benchmarks including ACAS Xu and a rocket lander controller. The result shows an obvious advantage over the state of the art.

AI.tldr: A novel neural network probabilistic verification framework that computes guaranteed safe probability ranges using probabilistic hulls, regression trees, and iterative refinement.
AI.motivation: Neural network probabilistic verification is crucial when inputs are affected by disturbances modeled by probabilistic variables, requiring computation of safe constraint satisfaction probabilities.
AI.method: Three innovations: (1) state space subdivision using regression trees to produce probabilistic hulls, (2) boundary-aware sampling to identify safety boundaries in input space, (3) iterative refinement with probabilistic prioritization for computing guaranteed safe probability ranges.
AI.result: The approach shows obvious advantage over state-of-the-art methods on various benchmarks including ACAS Xu and rocket lander controller, demonstrating accuracy and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully addresses neural network probabilistic verification by computing guaranteed safe probability ranges through efficient probabilistic hull construction and refinement techniques.

title: Understanding and Mitigating Spurious Signal Amplification in Test-Time Reinforcement Learning for Math Reasoning
summary: Test-time reinforcement learning (TTRL) always adapts models at inference time via pseudo-labeling, leaving it vulnerable to spurious optimization signals from label noise. Through an empirical study, we observe that responses with medium consistency form an ambiguity region and constitute the primary source of reward noise. Crucially, we find that such spurious signals can be even amplified through group-relative advantage estimation. Motivated by these findings, we propose a unified framework, Debiased and Denoised test-time Reinforcement Learning (DDRL), to mitigate spurious signals. Concretely, DDRL first applies a frequency-based sampling strategy to exclude ambiguous samples while maintaining a balanced set of positive and negative examples. It then adopts a debiased advantage estimation with fixed advantages, removing the bias introduced by group-relative policy optimization. Finally, DDRL incorporates a consensus-based off-policy refinement stage, which leverages the rejection-sampled dataset to enable efficient and stable model updates. Experiments on three large language models across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that DDRL consistently outperforms existing TTRL baselines. The code will soon be released at https://github.com/yuyongcan/DDRL.

AI.tldr: DDRL is a debiased and denoised test-time reinforcement learning framework that addresses spurious optimization signals from label noise by filtering ambiguous samples, using debiased advantage estimation, and incorporating consensus-based off-policy refinement.
AI.motivation: Test-time reinforcement learning suffers from spurious optimization signals caused by label noise, particularly from ambiguous samples in the "ambiguity region" where responses have medium consistency. These spurious signals are further amplified by group-relative advantage estimation, which motivates the need for a debiased and denoised approach.
AI.method: DDRL employs three key components: 1) Frequency-based sampling to exclude ambiguous samples while maintaining balanced positive/negative examples, 2) Debiased advantage estimation with fixed advantages to remove bias from group-relative policy optimization, and 3) Consensus-based off-policy refinement using rejection-sampled datasets for stable model updates.
AI.result: Experiments on three large language models across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that DDRL consistently outperforms existing TTRL baselines.
AI.conclusion: DDRL effectively mitigates spurious optimization signals in test-time reinforcement learning through a unified debiasing and denoising approach, demonstrating superior performance across various LLMs and mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: Separable Expert Architecture: Toward Privacy-Preserving LLM Personalization via Composable Adapters and Deletable User Proxies
summary: Current model training approaches incorporate user information directly into shared weights, making individual data removal computationally infeasible without retraining. This paper presents a three-layer architecture that decouples personal data from shared weights by combining a static base model, composable domain-expert LoRA adapters that shape behavior without imparting user data, and per-user proxy artefacts whose deletion constitutes deterministic unlearning. Evaluation on Phi-3.5-mini and Llama-3.1-8B confirms per-user differentiation in which personal data influences outputs while remaining isolated, verified by a return to baseline after proxy removal (KL divergence of approximately 0.21 nats, 82-89% verification pass rate) and near-zero cross-user contamination. Because user-specific information never enters shared weights, the architecture mitigates model inversion, membership inference, and training-data extraction against shared model components by construction. The approach converts machine unlearning from an intractable weight-editing problem into a deterministic deletion operation that preserves personalization alongside privacy-enhancing guarantees and is compatible with differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) for privacy-preserving shared model improvement.

AI.tldr: A three-layer architecture separates personal data from shared model weights using static base models, domain-expert LoRA adapters, and per-user proxy artifacts, enabling deterministic unlearning through proxy deletion while maintaining personalization and privacy.
AI.motivation: Current model training incorporates user information directly into shared weights, making individual data removal computationally infeasible and privacy violations difficult to prevent. There's a need for an architecture that enables deterministic data deletion (unlearning) while preserving personalization capabilities.
AI.method: Three-layer architecture: 1) Static base model, 2) Composable domain-expert LoRA adapters that shape behavior without containing user data, 3) Per-user proxy artifacts that contain personal data. User-specific information never enters shared weights - deletion of a user's proxy artifact constitutes deterministic unlearning.
AI.result: Evaluation on Phi-3.5-mini and Llama-3.1-8B shows successful per-user differentiation with personal data influencing outputs while remaining isolated. After proxy removal, models return to baseline (KL divergence ~0.21 nats, 82-89% verification pass rate) with near-zero cross-user contamination. The architecture mitigates model inversion, membership inference, and training-data extraction attacks against shared components.
AI.conclusion: The approach transforms machine unlearning from an intractable weight-editing problem into a simple deletion operation, preserving personalization alongside privacy guarantees. It's compatible with DP-SGD for privacy-preserving shared model improvement and offers a practical solution for data removal requirements while maintaining model utility.

title: Sub-Token Routing in LoRA for Adaptation and Query-Aware KV Compression
summary: Sub-token routing offers a finer control axis for transformer efficiency than the coarse units used in most prior work, such as tokens, pages, heads, or layers. In this paper, we study routing within a token representation itself in LoRA-adapted transformers. The motivation is that a relevant token need not be internally uniform: under a retention budget, preserved value groups are distributed unevenly both across tokens and within tokens, which suggests that KV compression need not be an all-or-nothing decision at token level. We study this fine-grained routing mechanism in two settings. For compression-aware language modeling, we introduce a query-independent design that combines routed subspace LoRA with value-group routing on the KV path. For downstream-task-preserving KV compression, we introduce a query-aware design in which a predictor-based selector allocates a global retention budget over context-token/value-group pairs using query-conditioned relevance. Experiments show that the query-independent design improves the quality-compression tradeoff for language modeling, while the query-aware design preserves downstream behavior under reduced KV budgets. We further examine the relation between token-level and sub-token-level query-aware routing, and show that they form complementary compression axes: token-level methods determine which tokens survive globally, while sub-token routing determines how the surviving tokens are compressed internally.

AI.tldr: Sub-token routing in LoRA-adapted transformers enables finer-grained KV compression by routing within token representations rather than making all-or-nothing decisions at token level, improving efficiency through query-independent designs for language modeling and query-aware designs for downstream task preservation.
AI.motivation: Traditional transformer compression methods operate at coarse units (tokens, pages, heads, layers), but token representations are not internally uniform - relevant value groups within tokens are distributed unevenly. This suggests KV compression need not be all-or-nothing at token level, allowing for more fine-grained efficiency control.
AI.method: Two approaches: 1) Query-independent design combining routed subspace LoRA with value-group routing on KV path for compression-aware language modeling. 2) Query-aware design using predictor-based selector to allocate global retention budget over context-token/value-group pairs using query-conditioned relevance for downstream-task-preserving KV compression.
AI.result: Query-independent design improves quality-compression tradeoff for language modeling. Query-aware design preserves downstream behavior under reduced KV budgets. Token-level and sub-token-level query-aware routing form complementary compression axes: token-level determines which tokens survive globally, while sub-token routing determines how surviving tokens are compressed internally.
AI.conclusion: Sub-token routing provides a finer control axis for transformer efficiency than traditional coarse units, enabling more nuanced KV compression decisions. The proposed methods demonstrate practical benefits across different scenarios, showing that fine-grained routing within token representations is a valuable direction for transformer optimization.

title: CoFEE: Reasoning Control for LLM-Based Feature Discovery
summary: Feature discovery from complex unstructured data is fundamentally a reasoning problem: it requires identifying abstractions that are predictive of a target outcome while avoiding leakage, proxies, and post-outcome signals. With the introduction of ever-improving Large Language Models (LLMs), our method provides a structured method for addressing this challenge. LLMs are well suited for this task by being able to process large amounts of information, but unconstrained feature generation can lead to weak features. In this work, we study reasoning control in LLMs by inducing cognitive behaviors for improving feature discovery. We introduce CoFEE (Cognitive Feature Engineering Engine), a reasoning control framework that enforces cognitive behaviors in how the LLM reasons during feature discovery. From a machine learning perspective, these cognitive behaviors act as structured inductive biases over the space of candidate features generated by the model. These behaviors have been exploited with success in ML models, and include backward chaining from outcomes, subgoal decomposition, verification against observability and leakage criteria, and explicit backtracking of rejected reasoning paths. In a controlled comparison, we show that enforcing cognitive behaviors yields features with higher empirical predictability than those under unconstrained vanilla LLM prompts. CoFEE achieves an average Success Rate Score that is 15.2% higher than the vanilla approach, while generating 29% fewer features and reducing costs by 53.3%. Using held-out feature evaluation, we assess whether cognitively induced features generalize beyond the data used for discovery. Our results indicate that, in our evaluated setting, reasoning control is associated with improvements in quality and efficiency of LLM-based feature discovery.

AI.tldr: CoFEE (Cognitive Feature Engineering Engine) is a framework that applies reasoning control to LLMs for feature discovery by enforcing cognitive behaviors like backward chaining and verification, resulting in higher quality features with reduced generation and costs.
AI.motivation: Feature discovery from complex unstructured data requires identifying predictive abstractions while avoiding leakage and proxies. While LLMs can process large amounts of information, unconstrained feature generation often leads to weak features, necessitating structured reasoning control.
AI.method: The authors introduce CoFEE, a reasoning control framework that enforces cognitive behaviors in LLMs during feature discovery. These behaviors include backward chaining from outcomes, subgoal decomposition, verification against observability and leakage criteria, and explicit backtracking of rejected reasoning paths.
AI.result: CoFEE achieves 15.2% higher Success Rate Score than vanilla LLM prompts, generates 29% fewer features, and reduces costs by 53.3%. Held-out feature evaluation shows that cognitively induced features generalize beyond the data used for discovery.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning control through cognitive behaviors significantly improves the quality and efficiency of LLM-based feature discovery, providing structured inductive biases that yield more predictive features while reducing generation costs.

title: Decoupled Travel Planning with Behavior Forest
summary: Behavior sequences, composed of executable steps, serve as the operational foundation for multi-constraint planning problems such as travel planning. In such tasks, each planning step is not only constrained locally but also influenced by global constraints spanning multiple subtasks, leading to a tightly coupled and complex decision process. Existing travel planning methods typically rely on a single decision space that entangles all subtasks and constraints, failing to distinguish between locally acting constraints within a subtask and global constraints that span multiple subtasks. Consequently, the model is forced to jointly reason over local and global constraints at each decision step, increasing the reasoning burden and reducing planning efficiency. To address this problem, we propose the Behavior Forest method. Specifically, our approach structures the decision-making process into a forest of parallel behavior trees, where each behavior tree is responsible for a subtask. A global coordination mechanism is introduced to orchestrate the interactions among these trees, enabling modular and coherent travel planning. Within this framework, large language models are embedded as decision engines within behavior tree nodes, performing localized reasoning conditioned on task-specific constraints to generate candidate subplans and adapt decisions based on coordination feedback. The behavior trees, in turn, provide an explicit control structure that guides LLM generation. This design decouples complex tasks and constraints into manageable subspaces, enabling task-specific reasoning and reducing the cognitive load of LLM. Experimental results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 6.67% on the TravelPlanner and by 11.82% on the ChinaTravel benchmarks, demonstrating its effectiveness in increasing LLM performance for complex multi-constraint travel planning.

AI.tldr: Behavior Forest method uses parallel behavior trees with global coordination to decouple multi-constraint travel planning into manageable subtasks, improving LLM performance by 6.67-11.82% over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Existing travel planning methods use a single decision space that entangles all subtasks and constraints, forcing models to jointly reason over local and global constraints at each step. This increases reasoning burden and reduces planning efficiency, as current approaches fail to distinguish between local constraints within subtasks and global constraints spanning multiple subtasks.
AI.method: Proposes Behavior Forest method that structures decision-making into a forest of parallel behavior trees, where each tree handles a subtask. Introduces global coordination mechanism to orchestrate interactions among trees. Embeds LLMs as decision engines within behavior tree nodes to perform localized reasoning conditioned on task-specific constraints, generating candidate subplans and adapting based on coordination feedback.
AI.result: Experimental results show the method outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 6.67% on TravelPlanner benchmark and by 11.82% on ChinaTravel benchmark, demonstrating effectiveness in increasing LLM performance for complex multi-constraint travel planning.
AI.conclusion: The Behavior Forest method successfully decouples complex tasks and constraints into manageable subspaces, enabling task-specific reasoning and reducing LLM cognitive load, leading to significant performance improvements in multi-constraint travel planning tasks.

title: To See the Unseen: on the Generalization Ability of Transformers in Symbolic Reasoning
summary: We investigate the ability of decoder-only transformer models to perform abstract symbolic reasoning; specifically solving propositional logic reasoning problems given in-context. Previous work demonstrated that models fail to generalize to problems involving variable names that were not observed during training, and it was shown that one reason behind this is the difficulty of copying (or generating) unseen tokens. We show both theoretically and empirically that a particular representational collapse also has a crucial role: the unembeddings (last-layer weights) of unseen tokens collapse to nearly the same vector during training. The collapse makes distinguishing multiple unseen variables difficult for the model (especially when the embedding and unembedding parameters are shared), and provides a mechanistic explanation for the effectiveness of existing heuristic interventions like "active forgetting", which periodically reset the token (un)embeddings. Based on these observations, we devise a combination of techniques, involving a small architecture change facilitating copying, data diversity, and freezing or resetting (un)embeddings, that achieves generalization to unseen tokens. We support our claims with extensive controlled experiments on propositional logic reasoning problems. Beyond synthetic experiments, we also observe evidence of (un)embedding collapse in the open-weight models in the Gemma 3 family, which includes 99 unused tokens reserved for downstream use. Empirically we find that the correlated embeddings of these tokens are a poor initialization for finetuning applications.

AI.tldr: Decoder-only transformers struggle with abstract symbolic reasoning using unseen tokens due to "unembedding collapse" where unseen tokens' final layer weights converge to similar vectors, making them indistinguishable. The paper proposes architecture changes, data diversity, and embedding reset techniques to solve this.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses why decoder-only transformer models fail to generalize to symbolic reasoning problems involving variable names not seen during training. Previous work identified token copying difficulties, but this paper identifies representational collapse of unseen tokens' unembeddings as a crucial additional factor limiting generalization.
AI.method: The authors combine theoretical analysis and empirical experiments on propositional logic reasoning problems. They propose techniques including: 1) small architecture changes to facilitate copying unseen tokens, 2) increased data diversity, and 3) freezing or resetting (un)embeddings to prevent collapse. They validate with controlled experiments and also examine real-world models (Gemma 3 family) for evidence of this phenomenon.
AI.result: The proposed techniques successfully achieve generalization to unseen tokens in propositional logic reasoning. The authors provide mechanistic explanation for why "active forgetting" (periodic resetting of token embeddings) works. They also find evidence of unembedding collapse in Gemma 3 models, showing that the correlated embeddings of unused tokens provide poor initialization for finetuning.
AI.conclusion: Unembedding collapse is a significant barrier to transformer generalization on symbolic reasoning with unseen tokens. The identified techniques—architectural modifications for copying, diverse training data, and embedding management—effectively address this limitation. The findings have practical implications for model design and finetuning strategies in real-world LLMs.

title: mcdok at SemEval-2026 Task 13: Finetuning LLMs for Detection of Machine-Generated Code
summary: Multi-domain detection of the machine-generated code snippets in various programming languages is a challenging task. SemEval-2026 Task~13 copes with this challenge in various angles, as a binary detection problem as well as attribution of the source. Specifically, its subtasks also cover generator LLM family detection, as well as a hybrid code co-generated by humans and machines, or adversarially modified codes hiding its origin. Our submitted systems adjusted the existing mdok approach (focused on machine-generated text detection) to these specific kinds of problems by exploring various base models, more suitable for code understanding. The results indicate that the submitted systems are competitive in all three subtasks. However, the margins from the top-performing systems are significant, and thus further improvements are possible.

AI.tldr: A system adaptation for detecting machine-generated code snippets across multiple programming languages and subtasks, achieving competitive but not top-tier performance in SemEval-2026 Task 13.
AI.motivation: Multi-domain detection of machine-generated code snippets is challenging due to various programming languages and detection angles needed, including binary detection, source attribution, LLM family identification, and detection of hybrid human-machine or adversarially modified code.
AI.method: Adapted existing mdok approach (originally for machine-generated text detection) to code-specific problems by exploring various base models more suitable for code understanding to handle the three subtasks.
AI.result: Submitted systems achieved competitive performance in all three subtasks (binary detection, source attribution, and hybrid/adversarial code detection), but with significant margins from top-performing systems indicating room for improvement.
AI.conclusion: While the adapted approach shows promise for multi-domain code snippet detection, further improvements are needed to close the gap with top-performing systems in the SemEval-2026 Task 13 competition.

title: GS-Quant: Granular Semantic and Generative Structural Quantization for Knowledge Graph Completion
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown immense potential in Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC), yet bridging the modality gap between continuous graph embeddings and discrete LLM tokens remains a critical challenge. While recent quantization-based approaches attempt to align these modalities, they typically treat quantization as flat numerical compression, resulting in semantically entangled codes that fail to mirror the hierarchical nature of human reasoning. In this paper, we propose GS-Quant, a novel framework that generates semantically coherent and structurally stratified discrete codes for KG entities. Unlike prior methods, GS-Quant is grounded in the insight that entity representations should follow a linguistic coarse-to-fine logic. We introduce a Granular Semantic Enhancement module that injects hierarchical knowledge into the codebook, ensuring that earlier codes capture global semantic categories while later codes refine specific attributes. Furthermore, a Generative Structural Reconstruction module imposes causal dependencies on the code sequence, transforming independent discrete units into structured semantic descriptors. By expanding the LLM vocabulary with these learned codes, we enable the model to reason over graph structures isomorphically to natural language generation. Experimental results demonstrate that GS-Quant significantly outperforms existing text-based and embedding-based baselines. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/mikumifa/GS-Quant.

AI.tldr: GS-Quant is a novel framework that generates hierarchical discrete codes for knowledge graph entities to bridge the modality gap between graph embeddings and LLM tokens, enabling better knowledge graph completion through semantically structured codes.
AI.motivation: There's a critical modality gap between continuous graph embeddings and discrete LLM tokens in knowledge graph completion. Existing quantization approaches treat quantization as flat numerical compression, resulting in semantically entangled codes that don't reflect the hierarchical nature of human reasoning.
AI.method: GS-Quant introduces: 1) Granular Semantic Enhancement module that injects hierarchical knowledge into the codebook (earlier codes capture global semantic categories, later codes refine specific attributes), and 2) Generative Structural Reconstruction module that imposes causal dependencies on the code sequence, transforming independent discrete units into structured semantic descriptors.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that GS-Quant significantly outperforms existing text-based and embedding-based baselines for knowledge graph completion.
AI.conclusion: GS-Quant successfully bridges the modality gap by generating semantically coherent and structurally stratified discrete codes that mirror human hierarchical reasoning, enabling LLMs to reason over graph structures isomorphically to natural language generation.

title: Channel-Free Human Activity Recognition via Inductive-Bias-Aware Fusion Design for Heterogeneous IoT Sensor Environments
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables, Preprint. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Human activity recognition (HAR) in Internet of Things (IoT) environments must cope with heterogeneous sensor settings that vary across datasets, devices, body locations, sensing modalities, and channel compositions. This heterogeneity makes conventional channel-fixed models difficult to reuse across sensing environments because their input representations are tightly coupled to predefined channel structures. To address this problem, we investigate strict channel-free HAR, in which a single shared model performs inference without assuming a fixed number, order, or semantic arrangement of input channels, and without relying on sensor-specific input layers or dataset-specific channel templates. We argue that fusion design is the central issue in this setting. Accordingly, we propose a channel-free HAR framework that combines channel-wise encoding with a shared encoder, metadata-conditioned late fusion via conditional batch normalization, and joint optimization of channel-level and fused predictions through a combination loss. The proposed model processes each channel independently to handle varying channel configurations, while sensor metadata such as body location, modality, and axis help recover structural information that channel-independent processing alone cannot retain. In addition, the joint loss encourages both the discriminability of individual channels and the consistency of the final fused prediction. Experiments on PAMAP2, together with robustness analysis on six HAR datasets, ablation studies, sensitivity analysis, efficiency evaluation, and cross-dataset transfer learning, demonstrate three main findings...

AI.tldr: A channel-free HAR framework for IoT environments that handles heterogeneous sensor settings by processing channels independently and fusing them using metadata-conditioned fusion.
AI.motivation: Human activity recognition in IoT environments faces challenges with heterogeneous sensor settings (different datasets, devices, body locations, modalities, channel compositions), making conventional channel-fixed models difficult to reuse across different sensing environments.
AI.method: Proposes a channel-free HAR framework with: 1) channel-wise encoding with shared encoder, 2) metadata-conditioned late fusion via conditional batch normalization, 3) joint optimization of channel-level and fused predictions through combination loss. Each channel is processed independently while sensor metadata helps recover structural information.
AI.result: Experiments on PAMAP2 with robustness analysis on six HAR datasets, ablation studies, sensitivity analysis, efficiency evaluation, and cross-dataset transfer learning demonstrate the framework's effectiveness in handling heterogeneous sensor settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed channel-free approach effectively addresses heterogeneity in IoT sensing environments, enabling single shared models to work across varying channel configurations without requiring fixed input structures or dataset-specific adaptations.

title: Enabling and Inhibitory Pathways of University Students' Willingness to Disclose AI Use: A Cognition-Affect-Conation Perspective
summary: The increasing integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in higher education has raised important questions regarding students' transparency in reporting AI-assisted work. This study investigates the psychological mechanisms underlying university students' willingness to disclose AI use by applying the Cognition--Affect--Conation (CAC) framework. A sequential explanatory mixed-methods design was employed. In the quantitative phase, survey data were collected from 546 university students and analysed using structural equation modelling to examine the relationships among cognitive perceptions, affective responses, and disclosure intention. In the qualitative phase, semi-structured interviews with 22 students were conducted to further interpret the quantitative findings. The results indicate that psychological safety significantly increases students' willingness to disclose AI use and is positively shaped by perceived fairness, perceived teacher support, and perceived organisational support. Conversely, evaluation apprehension reduces disclosure intention and psychological safety, and is strengthened by perceived stigma, perceived uncertainty, and privacy concern. Qualitative findings further reveal that institutional clarity and supportive instructional practices encourage openness, whereas policy ambiguity and fear of negative evaluation often lead students to adopt cautious or strategic disclosure practices. Overall, the study highlights the dual role of enabling and inhibitory psychological mechanisms in shaping AI-use disclosure and underscores the importance of supportive institutional environments and clear guidance for promoting responsible AI transparency in higher education.

AI.tldr: This study applies the Cognition-Affect-Conation framework to investigate psychological factors influencing university students' willingness to disclose AI use, finding that psychological safety and institutional support encourage transparency while evaluation apprehension and policy ambiguity inhibit it.
AI.motivation: The increasing integration of AI in higher education raises important questions about students' transparency in reporting AI-assisted work, necessitating investigation into the psychological mechanisms underlying disclosure intentions.
AI.method: Sequential explanatory mixed-methods design: quantitative phase with survey data from 546 students analyzed via structural equation modeling; qualitative phase with semi-structured interviews of 22 students to interpret quantitative findings.
AI.result: Psychological safety increases disclosure intention and is positively shaped by perceived fairness, teacher support, and organizational support. Evaluation apprehension reduces disclosure and psychological safety, strengthened by perceived stigma, uncertainty, and privacy concerns. Institutional clarity and supportive practices encourage openness while policy ambiguity and fear of negative evaluation lead to cautious disclosure.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights dual enabling and inhibitory psychological mechanisms in AI-use disclosure, emphasizing the importance of supportive institutional environments and clear guidance for promoting responsible AI transparency in higher education.

title: Relocation of compact sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ by diffeomorphisms and linear separability of datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$
summary:
Relocation of compact sets in an $n$-dimensional manifold by self-diffeomorphism is of its own interest as well as significant potential applications to data classification in data science. This paper presents a theory for relocating a finite number of compact sets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ to be relocated to arbitrary target domains in $\mathbb{R}^n$ by diffeomorphisms of $\mathbb{R}^n$. Furthermore, we prove that for any such collection, there exists a differentiable embedding into $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ such that their images become linearly separable.
  As applications of the established theory, we show that a finite number of compact datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ can be made linearly separable by width-$n$ deep neural networks (DNNs) with Leaky-ReLU, ELU, or SELU activation functions, under a mild condition. In addition, we show that any finite number of mutually disjoint compact datasets in $\mathbb{R}^n$ can be made linearly separable in $\mathbb{R}^{n+1}$ by a width-$(n+1)$ DNN.

AI.tldr: Compact sets in ℝⁿ can be relocated by diffeomorphisms to arbitrary target domains, and finite collections of disjoint compact datasets can be made linearly separable in ℝⁿ⁺¹ via neural networks.
AI.motivation: Relocating compact sets via self-diffeomorphisms is mathematically interesting and has practical applications in data science, particularly for making datasets linearly separable for classification tasks.
AI.method: Develops a mathematical theory for relocating finite collections of compact sets in ℝⁿ using diffeomorphisms, and proves that any such collection can be embedded into ℝⁿ⁺¹ to achieve linear separability. Applies this to neural networks with Leaky-ReLU, ELU, or SELU activations.
AI.result: Proves that finite compact datasets in ℝⁿ can be made linearly separable by width-n DNNs under mild conditions, and that disjoint compact datasets can be made linearly separable in ℝⁿ⁺¹ by width-(n+1) DNNs.
AI.conclusion: Provides a mathematical framework connecting diffeomorphism-based relocation of compact sets to neural network capability for linear separation, offering theoretical guarantees for data classification in machine learning.

title: Bridging the Training-Deployment Gap: Gated Encoding and Multi-Scale Refinement for Efficient Quantization-Aware Image Enhancement
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures. Accepted at the Mobile AI (MAI) 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Image enhancement models for mobile devices often struggle to balance high output quality with the fast processing speeds required by mobile hardware. While recent deep learning models can enhance low-quality mobile photos into high-quality images, their performance is often degraded when converted to lower-precision formats for actual use on mobile phones. To address this training-deployment mismatch, we propose an efficient image enhancement model designed specifically for mobile deployment. Our approach uses a hierarchical network architecture with gated encoder blocks and multiscale refinement to preserve fine-grained visual features. Moreover, we incorporate Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to simulate the effects of low-precision representation during the training process. This allows the network to adapt and prevents the typical drop in quality seen with standard post-training quantization (PTQ). Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method produces high-fidelity visual output while maintaining the low computational overhead needed for practical use on standard mobile devices. The code will be available at https://github.com/GenAI4E/QATIE.git.

AI.tldr: A mobile-friendly image enhancement model using hierarchical architecture with gated encoder blocks, multiscale refinement, and quantization-aware training to maintain high quality while being efficient for mobile deployment.
AI.motivation: Mobile image enhancement models face a dilemma between output quality and processing speed. Current deep learning models degrade when converted to lower-precision formats for mobile deployment, creating a training-deployment mismatch that needs to be addressed.
AI.method: Proposes an efficient mobile image enhancement model with hierarchical network architecture using gated encoder blocks and multiscale refinement to preserve fine-grained features. Incorporates Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to simulate low-precision representation during training, preventing quality degradation seen with standard post-training quantization.
AI.result: The proposed method produces high-fidelity visual output while maintaining low computational overhead suitable for standard mobile devices. It successfully addresses the training-deployment mismatch problem that typically causes quality degradation in mobile deployments.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a mobile-specific image enhancement solution that bridges the gap between training and deployment through quantization-aware training and efficient architecture design, enabling high-quality image enhancement on mobile hardware with practical processing speeds.

title: Supervised Learning Has a Necessary Geometric Blind Spot: Theory, Consequences, and Minimal Repair
comment: 29 pages. Code: https://github.com/vishalstark512/PMH. Preprint, not peer-reviewed. Affiliation: KU Leuven, Belgium
summary:
We prove that empirical risk minimisation (ERM) imposes a necessary geometric constraint on learned representations: any encoder that minimises supervised loss must retain non-zero Jacobian sensitivity in directions that are label-correlated in training data but nuisance at test time. This is not a contingent failure of current methods; it is a mathematical consequence of the supervised objective itself. We call this the geometric blind spot of supervised learning (Theorem 1), and show it holds across proper scoring rules, architectures, and dataset sizes.
  This single theorem unifies four lines of prior empirical work that were previously treated separately: non-robust predictive features, texture bias, corruption fragility, and the robustness-accuracy tradeoff. In this framing, adversarial vulnerability is one consequence of a broader structural fact about supervised learning geometry.
  We introduce Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI), a diagnostic that measures the theorem's bounded quantity directly, and show why common alternatives miss the key failure mode. PGD adversarial training reaches Jacobian Frobenius 2.91 yet has the worst clean-input geometry (TDI 1.336), while PMH achieves TDI 0.904. TDI is the only metric that detects this dissociation because it measures isotropic path-length distortion -- the exact quantity Theorem 1 bounds.
  Across seven vision tasks, BERT/SST-2, and ImageNet ViT-B/16 backbones used by CLIP, DINO, and SAM, the blind spot is measurable and repairable. It is present at foundation-model scale, worsens monotonically across language-model sizes (blind-spot ratio 0.860 to 0.765 to 0.742 from 66M to 340M), and is amplified by task-specific ERM fine-tuning (+54%), while PMH repairs it by 11x with one additional training term whose Gaussian form Proposition 5 proves is the unique perturbation law that uniformly penalises the encoder Jacobian.

AI.tldr: Supervised learning has an inherent geometric blind spot: ERM forces models to retain sensitivity to label-correlated but test-time-nuisance features, causing fundamental vulnerabilities that unify prior empirical findings like adversarial fragility and texture bias.
AI.motivation: Previous work identified various empirical phenomena like adversarial vulnerability, texture bias, corruption fragility, and the robustness-accuracy tradeoff, but treated them as separate issues. The paper aims to show these are all manifestations of a deeper mathematical constraint inherent to supervised learning itself.
AI.method: 1. Prove Theorem 1 establishing the geometric blind spot - that any ERM-trained encoder must retain non-zero Jacobian sensitivity to label-correlated but test-time-nuisance features. 2. Introduce Trajectory Deviation Index (TDI) to measure the bounded quantity from Theorem 1. 3. Evaluate across diverse architectures (vision models, BERT, ViT backbones) and scales. 4. Propose and implement PMH (Proposition 5 proves its Gaussian form is unique perturbation law penalizing encoder Jacobian uniformly).
AI.result: The blind spot is measurable across all tested models: present at foundation-model scale, worsens with larger language models (0.860 to 0.742 ratio from 66M to 340M), amplified by task-specific fine-tuning (+54%). PGD adversarial training reaches Jacobian Frobenius 2.91 but has worst clean-input geometry (TDI 1.336), while PMH achieves TDI 0.904. TDI is the only metric detecting this dissociation. PMH repairs the blind spot by 11x with one additional training term.
AI.conclusion: Supervised learning has an inherent geometric limitation (Theorem 1) that unifies previously disparate empirical findings. This blind spot causes fundamental vulnerabilities that current methods (even adversarial training) don't address properly. The proposed PMH method offers a principled solution by uniformly penalizing the encoder Jacobian, and the geometric framework provides new diagnostic tools (TDI) for understanding model vulnerabilities.

title: Thinking with Reasoning Skills: Fewer Tokens, More Accuracy
comment: 10 pages, The 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics -- Industry Track
summary: Reasoning LLMs often spend substantial tokens on long intermediate reasoning traces (e.g., chain-of-thought) when solving new problems. We propose to summarize and store reusable reasoning skills distilled from extensive deliberation and trial-and-error exploration, and to retrieve these skills at inference time to guide future reasoning. Unlike the prevailing \emph{reasoning from scratch} paradigm, our approach first recalls relevant skills for each query, helping the model avoid redundant detours and focus on effective solution paths. We evaluate our method on coding and mathematical reasoning tasks, and find that it significantly reduces reasoning tokens while improving overall performance. The resulting lower per-request cost indicates strong practical and economic potential for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: Reasoning LLMs can be made more efficient by pre-distilling reusable reasoning skills from deliberation, then retrieving them during inference to avoid redundant reasoning steps, reducing token usage while improving performance.
AI.motivation: Current reasoning LLMs waste substantial tokens on long intermediate reasoning traces (like chain-of-thought) when solving new problems, leading to inefficient and costly reasoning processes.
AI.method: Proposes summarizing and storing reusable reasoning skills distilled from extensive deliberation and trial-and-error exploration, then retrieving these skills at inference time to guide future reasoning rather than reasoning from scratch.
AI.result: Evaluation on coding and mathematical reasoning tasks shows significant reduction in reasoning tokens while improving overall performance, with lower per-request cost indicating strong practical and economic potential.
AI.conclusion: A skill-based reasoning approach that retrieves pre-learned reasoning patterns offers a more efficient alternative to the prevailing "reasoning from scratch" paradigm, with clear benefits for real-world deployment.

title: Even More Guarantees for Variational Inference in the Presence of Symmetries
comment: Accepted for presentation at the OPTIMAL Workshop at AISTATS 2026
summary: When approximating an intractable density via variational inference (VI) the variational family is typically chosen as a simple parametric family that very likely does not contain the target. This raises the question: Under which conditions can we recover characteristics of the target despite misspecification? In this work, we extend previous results on robust VI with location-scale families under target symmetries. We derive sufficient conditions guaranteeing exact recovery of the mean when using the forward Kullback-Leibler divergence and $α$-divergences. We further show how and why optimization can fail to recover the target mean in the absence of our sufficient conditions, providing initial guidelines on the choice of the variational family and $α$-value.

AI.tldr: Robust variational inference can recover target distribution characteristics despite model misspecification under certain conditions with location-scale families and target symmetries.
AI.motivation: Variational inference typically uses simple parametric families that likely don't contain the target distribution (misspecification). The paper investigates when we can still recover target characteristics despite this misspecification.
AI.method: Extends previous work on robust VI with location-scale families under target symmetries. Derives sufficient conditions for exact mean recovery using forward Kullback-Leibler divergence and α-divergences.
AI.result: Establishes sufficient conditions that guarantee exact recovery of the target mean. Also shows how optimization can fail to recover the mean when these conditions are absent, providing guidelines for choosing variational family and α-value.
AI.conclusion: Robust VI can successfully recover target characteristics under certain symmetry conditions and divergence choices, with practical implications for variational family selection and α-parameter optimization.

title: Who Defines "Best"? Towards Interactive, User-Defined Evaluation of LLM Leaderboards
comment: Accepted to the 2026 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT 2026)
summary: LLM leaderboards are widely used to compare models and guide deployment decisions. However, leaderboard rankings are shaped by evaluation priorities set by benchmark designers, rather than by the diverse goals and constraints of actual users and organizations. A single aggregate score often obscures how models behave across different prompt types and compositions. In this work, we conduct an in-depth analysis of the dataset used in the LMArena (formerly Chatbot Arena) benchmark and investigate this evaluation challenge by designing an interactive visualization interface as a design probe. Our analysis reveals that the dataset is heavily skewed toward certain topics, that model rankings vary across prompt slices, and that preference-based judgments are used in ways that blur their intended scope. Building on this analysis, we introduce a visualization interface that allows users to define their own evaluation priorities by selecting and weighting prompt slices and to explore how rankings change accordingly. A qualitative study suggests that this interactive approach improves transparency and supports more context-specific model evaluation, pointing toward alternative ways to design and use LLM leaderboards.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques current LLM leaderboards for imposing benchmark designers' priorities rather than user needs, proposes an interactive visualization tool that allows custom ranking based on user-selected prompt slices and weights, and shows this improves evaluation transparency and context-specificity.
AI.motivation: Current LLM leaderboards fail to reflect diverse user goals and constraints because they use single aggregate scores that obscure model performance across different prompt types and compositions. Benchmark designers' priorities dominate rather than actual user needs, limiting leaderboard utility for deployment decisions.
AI.method: Analyzed the LMArena (Chatbot Arena) benchmark dataset to reveal biases; designed an interactive visualization interface as a design probe allowing users to select and weight prompt slices; conducted qualitative study to evaluate the interface's effectiveness.
AI.result: Found dataset heavily skewed toward certain topics; model rankings vary significantly across prompt slices; preference-based judgments are used inconsistently with intended scope. Interactive visualization enabled users to define custom evaluation priorities and see resulting ranking changes.
AI.conclusion: Interactive, user-driven evaluation approaches improve transparency and support context-specific model assessment, suggesting alternative designs for LLM leaderboards that better serve diverse user needs rather than imposing a single ranking system.

title: A Green-Integral-Constrained Neural Solver with Stochastic Physics-Informed Regularization
summary: Standard physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) struggle to simulate highly oscillatory Helmholtz solutions in heterogeneous media because pointwise minimization of second-order PDE residuals is computationally expensive, biased toward smooth solutions, and requires artificial absorbing boundary layers to restrict the solution. To overcome these challenges, we introduce a Green-Integral (GI) neural solver for the acoustic Helmholtz equation. It departs from the PDE-residual-based formulation by enforcing wave physics through an integral representation that imposes a nonlocal constraint. Oscillatory behavior and outgoing radiation are encoded directly through the integral kernel, eliminating second-order spatial derivatives and enforcing physical solutions without additional boundary layers. Theoretically, optimizing this GI loss via a neural network acts as a spectrally tuned preconditioned iteration, enabling convergence in heterogeneous media where the classical Born series diverges. By exploiting FFT-based convolution to accelerate the GI loss evaluation, our approach substantially reduces GPU memory usage and training time. However, this efficiency relies on a fixed regular grid, which can limit local resolution. To improve local accuracy in strong scattering regions, we also propose a hybrid GI+PDE loss, enforcing a lightweight Helmholtz residual at a small number of nonuniformly sampled collocation points. We evaluate our method on seismic benchmark models characterized by structural contrasts and subwavelength heterogeneity at frequencies up to 20Hz. GI-based training consistently outperforms PDE-based PINNs, reducing computational cost by over a factor of ten. In models with localized scattering, the hybrid loss yields the most accurate reconstructions, providing a stable, efficient, and physically grounded alternative.

AI.tldr: A Green-Integral neural solver for acoustic Helmholtz equations that replaces pointwise PDE residuals with integral representations to efficiently handle highly oscillatory solutions in heterogeneous media.
AI.motivation: Standard PINNs struggle with highly oscillatory Helmholtz solutions in heterogeneous media due to expensive second-order derivative computations, bias toward smooth solutions, and need for artificial absorbing boundary layers.
AI.method: Introduces a Green-Integral (GI) neural solver that enforces wave physics through an integral representation instead of PDE residuals, encoding oscillatory behavior and radiation directly via integral kernel. Also proposes hybrid GI+PDE loss for improved local accuracy in strong scattering regions.
AI.result: GI-based training consistently outperforms PDE-based PINNs, reducing computational cost by over a factor of ten. Hybrid loss yields most accurate reconstructions in models with localized scattering.
AI.conclusion: The GI neural solver provides a stable, efficient, and physically grounded alternative for solving acoustic Helmholtz equations in heterogeneous media, overcoming limitations of traditional PINNs while enabling convergence where classical methods diverge.

title: Inferring High-Level Events from Timestamped Data: Complexity and Medical Applications
comment: This is the full version (with appendix) of a paper appearing at the 23rd International Conference on Principles of Knowledge Representation and Reasoning (KR 2026)
summary: In this paper, we develop a novel logic-based approach to detecting high-level temporally extended events from timestamped data and background knowledge. Our framework employs logical rules to capture existence and termination conditions for simple temporal events and to combine these into meta-events. In the medical domain, for example, disease episodes and therapies are inferred from timestamped clinical observations, such as diagnoses and drug administrations stored in patient records, and can be further combined into higher-level disease events. As some incorrect events might be inferred, we use constraints to identify incompatible combinations of events and propose a repair mechanism to select preferred consistent sets of events. While reasoning in the full framework is intractable, we identify relevant restrictions that ensure polynomial-time data complexity. Our prototype system implements core components of the approach using answer set programming. An evaluation on a lung cancer use case supports the interest of the approach, both in terms of computational feasibility and positive alignment of our results with medical expert opinions. While strongly motivated by the needs of the healthcare domain, our framework is purposely generic, enabling its reuse in other areas.

AI.tldr: A logic-based framework for detecting temporally extended events from timestamped data using logical rules, with medical domain application (disease episodes) and polynomial-time restrictions.
AI.motivation: Need to detect high-level temporally extended events from timestamped data in domains like healthcare, where disease episodes and therapies need to be inferred from clinical observations in patient records.
AI.method: Uses logical rules to capture existence/termination conditions for simple events, combines them into meta-events, employs constraints to identify incompatible event combinations, and proposes repair mechanisms for consistent event sets. Implements core components using answer set programming.
AI.result: Identified restrictions ensuring polynomial-time data complexity. Prototype evaluation on lung cancer use case shows computational feasibility and positive alignment with medical expert opinions.
