AI.conclusion: The framework is computationally feasible, validated in medical domain, and generically reusable for other applications requiring temporally extended event detection from timestamped data.

title: Tempered Sequential Monte Carlo for Trajectory and Policy Optimization with Differentiable Dynamics
summary: We propose a sampling-based framework for finite-horizon trajectory and policy optimization under differentiable dynamics by casting controller design as inference. Specifically, we minimize a KL-regularized expected trajectory cost, which yields an optimal "Boltzmann-tilted" distribution over controller parameters that concentrates on low-cost solutions as temperature decreases. To sample efficiently from this sharp, potentially multimodal target, we introduce tempered sequential Monte Carlo (TSMC): an annealing scheme that adaptively reweights and resamples particles along a tempering path from a prior to the target distribution, while using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo rejuvenation to maintain diversity and exploit exact gradients obtained by differentiating through trajectory rollouts. For policy optimization, we extend TSMC via (i) a deterministic empirical approximation of the initial-state distribution and (ii) an extended-space construction that treats rollout randomness as auxiliary variables. Experiments across trajectory- and policy-optimization benchmarks show that TSMC is broadly applicable and compares favorably to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: TSMC: A sampling-based optimization framework using tempered sequential Monte Carlo for trajectory and policy optimization under differentiable dynamics.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of optimizing controllers under differentiable dynamics, where traditional methods may struggle with sharp, multimodal optimization landscapes that concentrate on low-cost solutions as temperature decreases.
AI.method: The authors cast controller design as inference, minimizing a KL-regularized expected trajectory cost. They introduce Tempered Sequential Monte Carlo (TSMC), which uses annealing to adaptively reweight and resample particles along a tempering path from prior to target distribution, with Hamiltonian Monte Carlo rejuvenation for diversity and gradient exploitation.
AI.result: Experiments across trajectory- and policy-optimization benchmarks show TSMC is broadly applicable and compares favorably to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: TSMC provides an effective sampling-based framework for finite-horizon trajectory and policy optimization that handles challenging multimodal optimization landscapes through adaptive tempering and gradient exploitation.

title: Conditional anomaly detection with soft harmonic functions
comment: Published at IEEE International Conference on Data Mining (ICDM 2011). 10.1109/ICDM.2011.40
summary: In this paper, we consider the problem of conditional anomaly detection that aims to identify data instances with an unusual response or a class label. We develop a new non-parametric approach for conditional anomaly detection based on the soft harmonic solution, with which we estimate the confidence of the label to detect anomalous mislabeling. We further regularize the solution to avoid the detection of isolated examples and examples on the boundary of the distribution support. We demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed method on several synthetic and UCI ML datasets in detecting unusual labels when compared to several baseline approaches. We also evaluate the performance of our method on a real-world electronic health record dataset where we seek to identify unusual patient-management decisions.

AI.tldr: A novel non-parametric approach for conditional anomaly detection using soft harmonic solutions to identify unusual class labels, with regularization to avoid detecting isolated examples and boundary cases.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of conditional anomaly detection - identifying data instances with unusual responses or class labels, which is important for detecting anomalous mislabeling and unusual decision-making in various applications.
AI.method: Develops a non-parametric approach based on soft harmonic solution to estimate label confidence for detecting anomalous mislabeling. Regularizes the solution to avoid detecting isolated examples and examples on distribution boundaries.
AI.result: Demonstrates efficacy on synthetic and UCI ML datasets in detecting unusual labels, outperforming several baseline approaches. Also shows successful application on real-world electronic health record dataset for identifying unusual patient-management decisions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed soft harmonic solution approach provides an effective method for conditional anomaly detection, particularly for identifying unusual labels, with practical applications in domains like healthcare decision analysis.

title: Tool Attention Is All You Need: Dynamic Tool Gating and Lazy Schema Loading for Eliminating the MCP/Tools Tax in Scalable Agentic Workflows
summary: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) has become a common interface for connecting large language model (LLM) agents to external tools, but its reliance on stateless, eager schema injection imposes a hidden per-turn overhead the MCP Tax or Tools Tax that practitioner reports place between roughly 10k and 60k tokens in typical multi-server deployments. This payload inflates the key-value cache, is associated with reasoning degradation as context utilization approaches published fracture points around 70%, and turns token budgets into a recurring operational cost. We introduce Tool Attention, a middleware-layer mechanism that generalizes the "Attention Is All You Need" paradigm from self-attention over tokens to gated attention over tools. Tool Attention combines (i) an Intent Schema Overlap (ISO) score from sentence embeddings, (ii) a state-aware gating function enforcing preconditions and access scopes, and (iii) a two-phase lazy schema loader that keeps a compact summary pool in context and promotes full JSON schemas only for top-k gated tools. We evaluate on a simulated 120-tool, six-server benchmark whose per-server token counts are calibrated to public audits of real MCP deployments. In this simulation, Tool Attention directly reduces measured per-turn tool tokens by 95.0% (47.3k -> 2.4k) and raises effective context utilization (a token-ratio quantity) from 24% to 91%. End-to-end figures for task success, latency, cost, and reasoning quality are reported as projections derived from the measured token counts combined with published deployment telemetry; they are not measured on live LLM agents, and we mark projected values explicitly throughout. Taken together, the results support a simple thesis: protocol-level efficiency, not raw context length, is a binding constraint on scalable gentic systems. The code for this work is accessible at https://github.com/asadani/tool-attention

AI.tldr: Tool Attention reduces MCP's tool schema overhead by 95% through gated attention over tools instead of eager schema injection, improving context utilization from 24% to 91%.
AI.motivation: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) imposes a "Tools Tax" of 10k-60k tokens per turn in multi-server deployments due to stateless, eager schema injection. This overhead inflates key-value caches, causes reasoning degradation near 70% context utilization, and creates recurring operational costs from token budgets.
AI.method: Tool Attention generalizes the attention mechanism from tokens to tools using three components: (1) Intent Schema Overlap (ISO) score from sentence embeddings, (2) state-aware gating function enforcing preconditions and access scopes, and (3) two-phase lazy schema loader that keeps compact summaries in context and promotes full JSON schemas only for top-k gated tools.
AI.result: In a 120-tool, six-server benchmark simulation calibrated to real MCP deployments, Tool Attention reduces per-turn tool tokens by 95.0% (47.3k → 2.4k) and raises effective context utilization from 24% to 91%. Projected improvements are shown for task success, latency, cost, and reasoning quality, though these are derived from token measurements rather than live agent testing.
AI.conclusion: Protocol-level efficiency, not raw context length, is a binding constraint on scalable agent systems. Tool Attention demonstrates that intelligent tool management via attention mechanisms can dramatically reduce overhead and improve system performance.

title: Dynamical Priors as a Training Objective in Reinforcement Learning
comment: Supplementary material can be accessed here: https://github.com/drsukeshs/esd-rl
summary: Standard reinforcement learning (RL) optimizes policies for reward but imposes few constraints on how decisions evolve over time. As a result, policies may achieve high performance while exhibiting temporally incoherent behavior such as abrupt confidence shifts, oscillations, or degenerate inactivity. We introduce Dynamical Prior Reinforcement Learning (DP-RL), a training framework that augments policy gradient learning with an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics that implement evidence accumulation and hysteresis. Without modifying the reward, environment, or policy architecture, this prior shapes the temporal evolution of action probabilities during learning. Across three minimal environments, we show that dynamical priors systematically alter decision trajectories in task-dependent ways, promoting temporally structured behavior that cannot be explained by generic smoothing. These results demonstrate that training objectives alone can control the temporal geometry of decision-making in RL agents.

AI.tldr: DP-RL introduces a training framework that adds dynamical priors to reinforcement learning to shape temporal decision-making patterns without modifying rewards, environments, or policy architectures.
AI.motivation: Standard RL optimizes for reward but doesn't constrain temporal decision patterns, leading to temporally incoherent behavior like abrupt confidence shifts, oscillations, or degenerate inactivity in policies.
AI.method: Dynamical Prior Reinforcement Learning (DP-RL) augments policy gradient learning with an auxiliary loss derived from external state dynamics that implement evidence accumulation and hysteresis.
AI.result: Across three minimal environments, dynamical priors systematically alter decision trajectories in task-dependent ways, promoting temporally structured behavior that cannot be explained by generic smoothing.
AI.conclusion: Training objectives alone can control the temporal geometry of decision-making in RL agents, demonstrating that policy behavior can be shaped beyond just reward optimization.

title: Alignment has a Fantasia Problem
summary: Modern AI assistants are trained to follow instructions, implicitly assuming that users can clearly articulate their goals and the kind of assistance they need. Decades of behavioral research, however, show that people often engage with AI systems before their goals are fully formed. When AI systems treat prompts as complete expressions of intent, they can appear to be useful or convenient, but not necessarily aligned with the users' needs. We call these failures Fantasia interactions. We argue that Fantasia interactions demand a rethinking of alignment research: rather than treating users as rational oracles, AI should provide cognitive support by actively helping users form and refine their intent through time. This requires an interdisciplinary approach that bridges machine learning, interface design, and behavioral science. We synthesize insights from these fields to characterize the mechanisms and failures of Fantasia interactions. We then show why existing interventions are insufficient, and propose a research agenda for designing and evaluating AI systems that better help humans navigate uncertainty in their tasks.

AI.tldr: AI systems need to help users clarify vague or evolving intentions rather than treating initial prompts as complete expressions of intent, addressing what the authors call "Fantasia interactions"
AI.motivation: Current AI systems assume users can clearly articulate their goals and needs, but behavioral research shows people often engage with AI before their goals are fully formed, leading to misaligned interactions that appear useful but don't meet users' actual needs
AI.method: The paper takes an interdisciplinary approach that synthesizes insights from machine learning, interface design, and behavioral science to characterize the mechanisms and failures of Fantasia interactions
AI.result: The analysis shows why existing interventions are insufficient to address Fantasia interactions, revealing the gap between how AI systems currently operate and how users actually think and communicate their needs
AI.conclusion: The paper proposes a new research agenda for designing AI systems that provide cognitive support to help users form and refine their intent over time, fundamentally rethinking alignment to better help humans navigate task uncertainty

title: Drug Synergy Prediction via Residual Graph Isomorphism Networks and Attention Mechanisms
summary: In the treatment of complex diseases, treatment regimens using a single drug often yield limited efficacy and can lead to drug resistance. In contrast, combination drug therapies can significantly improve therapeutic outcomes through synergistic effects. However, experimentally validating all possible drug combinations is prohibitively expensive, underscoring the critical need for efficient computational prediction methods. Although existing approaches based on deep learning and graph neural networks (GNNs) have made considerable progress, challenges remain in reducing structural bias, improving generalization capability, and enhancing model interpretability. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a collaborative prediction graph neural network that integrates molecular structural features and cell-line genomic profiles with drug-drug interactions to enhance the prediction of synergistic effects. We introduce a novel model named the Residual Graph Isomorphism Network integrated with an Attention mechanism (ResGIN-Att). The model first extracts multi scale topological features of drug molecules using a residual graph isomorphism network, where residual connections help mitigate over-smoothing in deep layers. Subsequently, an adaptive Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) module fuses structural information from local to global scales. Finally, a cross-attention module is designed to explicitly model drug-drug interactions and identify key chemical substructures. Extensive experiments on five public benchmark datasets demonstrate that ResGIN-Att achieves competitive performance, comparing favorably against key baseline methods while exhibiting promising generalization capability and robustness.

AI.tldr: ResGIN-Att: A collaborative graph neural network that integrates drug molecular structures and cell-line genomic profiles with drug-drug interactions to predict synergistic drug combinations, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Single drug therapies often have limited efficacy and can lead to drug resistance in complex diseases. Combination therapies can improve outcomes through synergy, but experimental validation of all combinations is prohibitively expensive, creating need for computational prediction methods. Existing deep learning and GNN approaches still struggle with structural bias, generalization, and interpretability.
AI.method: Proposes ResGIN-Att (Residual Graph Isomorphism Network with Attention): 1) Extracts multi-scale topological drug features using residual graph isomorphism network with residual connections to prevent over-smoothing, 2) Uses adaptive LSTM module to fuse structural information from local to global scales, 3) Employs cross-attention module to explicitly model drug-drug interactions and identify key chemical substructures.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five public benchmark datasets show that ResGIN-Att achieves competitive performance compared to key baseline methods, demonstrating promising generalization capability and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ResGIN-Att model effectively addresses limitations of existing approaches by integrating molecular structural features, cell-line genomic profiles, and drug-drug interactions with attention mechanisms, providing a robust solution for predicting synergistic drug combinations with improved generalization and interpretability.

title: Bounding the Black Box: A Statistical Certification Framework for AI Risk Regulation
summary:
Artificial intelligence now decides who receives a loan, who is flagged for criminal investigation, and whether an autonomous vehicle brakes in time. Governments have responded: the EU AI Act, the NIST Risk Management Framework, and the Council of Europe Convention all demand that high-risk systems demonstrate safety before deployment. Yet beneath this regulatory consensus lies a critical vacuum: none specifies what ``acceptable risk'' means in quantitative terms, and none provides a technical method for verifying that a deployed system actually meets such a threshold. The regulatory architecture is in place; the verification instrument is not.
  This gap is not theoretical. As the EU AI Act moves into full enforcement, developers face mandatory conformity assessments without established methodologies for producing quantitative safety evidence - and the systems most in need of oversight are opaque statistical inference engines that resist white-box scrutiny.
  This paper provides the missing instrument. Drawing on the aviation certification paradigm, we propose a two-stage framework that transforms AI risk regulation into engineering practice. In Stage One, a competent authority formally fixes an acceptable failure probability $δ$ and an operational input domain $\varepsilon$ - a normative act with direct civil liability implications. In Stage Two, the RoMA and gRoMA statistical verification tools compute a definitive, auditable upper bound on the system's true failure rate, requiring no access to model internals and scaling to arbitrary architectures. We demonstrate how this certificate satisfies existing regulatory obligations, shifts accountability upstream to developers, and integrates with the legal frameworks that exist today.

AI.tldr: A framework for quantitative AI risk verification that fills the gap between AI regulations demanding safety proof and the lack of technical methods to measure "acceptable risk" - using statistical tools to certify systems meet failure probability thresholds without needing model access.
AI.motivation: Current AI regulations (EU AI Act, NIST Framework, Council of Europe Convention) require high-risk AI systems to demonstrate safety before deployment, but none specify quantitative definitions of "acceptable risk" or provide technical verification methods. This creates a critical gap as developers face mandatory conformity assessments without established methodologies for producing quantitative safety evidence, especially for opaque AI systems.
AI.method: Two-stage framework inspired by aviation certification: 1) Competent authority formally fixes acceptable failure probability (δ) and operational input domain (ε) - establishing normative standards with liability implications. 2) Statistical verification tools (RoMA and gRoMA) compute auditable upper bounds on system's true failure rate without requiring access to model internals, scaling to arbitrary architectures.
AI.result: Provides the missing verification instrument for AI risk regulation, enabling definitive certification that systems meet specified safety thresholds. The framework satisfies existing regulatory obligations, shifts accountability upstream to developers, and integrates with current legal frameworks.
AI.conclusion: This paper bridges the critical gap between AI safety regulations and technical verification by providing a practical engineering framework for quantitative risk assessment, transforming normative safety requirements into measurable, auditable compliance that can be implemented with existing regulatory structures.

title: Generalizing Numerical Reasoning in Table Data through Operation Sketches and Self-Supervised Learning
comment: Accepted to TACL. This is a pre-MIT Press publication version
summary: Numerical reasoning over expert-domain tables often exhibits high in-domain accuracy but limited robustness to domain shift. Models trained with supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on specific datasets tend to rely on header-operation shortcuts rather than structural reasoning. We introduce TaNOS, a continual pre-training framework comprising three components: (i) header anonymization to reduce lexical memorization, (ii) operation sketches that provide minimal structural cues, and (iii) self-supervised pretraining that constructs correctness-guaranteed program-question pairs from given tables in a program-first manner. By decoupling domain semantics and numerical operation structure, TaNOS improves the transferability of numerical reasoning. Applied to an 8B instruction-tuned model, TaNOS achieves 80.13% execution accuracy on FinQA with only 10% train data, outperforming SFT baseline (73.97%) with full train data and proprietary models such as GPT-5, Gemini-2.5-Pro. Furthermore, in the domain-shift experiments, TaNOS displays nearly-negligible cross-domain gap (<2pp) when standard SFT shows over 10pp gap. These results suggest that structural guidance with operation sketches, header-agnostic representations, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision can improve the robustness of numerical reasoning across diverse expert-domain tables.

AI.tldr: TaNOS is a continual pre-training framework that improves numerical reasoning robustness across expert-domain tables by decoupling domain semantics from structural reasoning through header anonymization, operation sketches, and self-supervised program-first training.
AI.motivation: Numerical reasoning models for expert-domain tables show high in-domain accuracy but poor robustness to domain shifts. Traditional supervised fine-tuning leads models to learn header-operation shortcuts rather than genuine structural reasoning, limiting transferability across domains.
AI.method: TaNOS introduces three components: (1) Header anonymization to reduce lexical memorization, (2) Operation sketches providing minimal structural cues, and (3) Self-supervised pretraining that constructs correctness-guaranteed program-question pairs from tables using a program-first approach, decoupling domain semantics from numerical operation structure.
AI.result: On FinQA with only 10% training data, TaNOS achieved 80.13% execution accuracy, outperforming SFT baseline (73.97%) using full data and proprietary models like GPT-5 and Gemini-2.5-Pro. In domain-shift experiments, TaNOS showed less than 2pp cross-domain gap versus over 10pp gap for standard SFT.
AI.conclusion: Structural guidance with operation sketches, header-agnostic representations, and correctness-guaranteed self-supervision significantly improves numerical reasoning robustness across diverse expert-domain tables, suggesting a promising direction for domain-agnostic table reasoning.

title: Nemobot Games: Crafting Strategic AI Gaming Agents for Interactive Learning with Large Language Models
summary: This paper introduces a new paradigm for AI game programming, leveraging large language models (LLMs) to extend and operationalize Claude Shannon's taxonomy of game-playing machines. Central to this paradigm is Nemobot, an interactive agentic engineering environment that enables users to create, customize, and deploy LLM-powered game agents while actively engaging with AI-driven strategies. The LLM-based chatbot, integrated within Nemobot, demonstrates its capabilities across four distinct classes of games. For dictionary-based games, it compresses state-action mappings into efficient, generalized models for rapid adaptability. In rigorously solvable games, it employs mathematical reasoning to compute optimal strategies and generates human-readable explanations for its decisions. For heuristic-based games, it synthesizes strategies by combining insights from classical minimax algorithms (see, e.g., shannon1950chess) with crowd-sourced data. Finally, in learning-based games, it utilizes reinforcement learning with human feedback and self-critique to iteratively refine strategies through trial-and-error and imitation learning. Nemobot amplifies this framework by offering a programmable environment where users can experiment with tool-augmented generation and fine-tuning of strategic game agents. From strategic games to role-playing games, Nemobot demonstrates how AI agents can achieve a form of self-programming by integrating crowdsourced learning and human creativity to iteratively refine their own logic. This represents a step toward the long-term goal of self-programming AI.

AI.tldr: Nemobot introduces an LLM-powered game agent creation environment that operationalizes Shannon's taxonomy, enabling AI agents to achieve self-programming through crowdsourced learning across four game classes.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to extend Claude Shannon's taxonomy of game-playing machines by leveraging modern LLMs to create a new paradigm for AI game programming, addressing the need for adaptable, explainable, and self-improving game agents.
AI.method: Developed Nemobot, an interactive agentic engineering environment that creates LLM-powered game agents using four distinct approaches: 1) state-action compression for dictionary games, 2) mathematical reasoning for solvable games, 3) heuristic synthesis combining minimax with crowdsourced data, and 4) reinforcement learning with human feedback for learning-based games.
AI.result: Nemobot demonstrates capabilities across four game classes, showing how AI agents can achieve self-programming by integrating crowdsourced learning and human creativity, representing progress toward self-programming AI.
AI.conclusion: This work presents a significant step toward self-programming AI through the Nemobot framework, which operationalizes Shannon's taxonomy using LLMs to create adaptable, explainable game agents that can refine their own logic through human-in-the-loop learning.

title: A temporal deep learning framework for calibration of low-cost air quality sensors
summary: Low-cost air quality sensors (LCS) provide a practical alternative to expensive regulatory-grade instruments, making dense urban monitoring networks possible. Yet their adoption is limited by calibration challenges, including sensor drift, environmental cross-sensitivity, and variability in performance from device to device. This work presents a deep learning framework for calibrating LCS measurements of PM$_{2.5}$, PM$_{10}$, and NO$_2$ using a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network, trained on co-located reference data from the OxAria network in Oxford, UK. Unlike the Random Forest (RF) baseline, which treats each observation independently, the proposed approach captures temporal dependencies and delayed environmental effects through sequence-based learning, achieving higher $R^2$ values across training, validation, and test sets for all three pollutants. A feature set is constructed combining time-lagged parameters, harmonic encodings, and interaction terms to improve generalization on unseen temporal windows. Validation of unseen calibrated values against the Equivalence Spreadsheet Tool 3.1 demonstrates regulatory compliance with expanded uncertainties of 22.11% for NO$_2$, 12.42% for PM$_{10}$, and 9.1% for PM$_{2.5}$.

AI.tldr: LSTM-based deep learning framework outperforms Random Forest for calibrating low-cost air quality sensors, achieving regulatory compliance for PM2.5, PM10, and NO2 measurements.
AI.motivation: Low-cost air quality sensors (LCS) enable dense urban monitoring networks but face calibration challenges including sensor drift, environmental cross-sensitivity, and device-to-device variability.
AI.method: Proposes a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network that captures temporal dependencies and delayed environmental effects through sequence-based learning, using co-located reference data from the OxAria network in Oxford, UK. Feature set includes time-lagged parameters, harmonic encodings, and interaction terms.
AI.result: Outperforms Random Forest baseline with higher R² values across all datasets. Achieves regulatory compliance with expanded uncertainties: 22.11% for NO₂, 12.42% for PM₁₀, and 9.1% for PM₂.₅ when validated against Equivalence Spreadsheet Tool 3.1.
AI.conclusion: The LSTM-based deep learning framework effectively addresses calibration challenges of low-cost air quality sensors, offering superior performance over traditional methods and enabling reliable deployment in dense urban monitoring networks.

title: From Research Question to Scientific Workflow: Leveraging Agentic AI for Science Automation
summary: Scientific workflow systems automate execution -- scheduling, fault tolerance, resource management -- but not the semantic translation that precedes it. Scientists still manually convert research questions into workflow specifications, a task requiring both domain knowledge and infrastructure expertise. We propose an agentic architecture that closes this gap through three layers: an LLM interprets natural language into structured intents (semantic layer); validated generators produce reproducible workflow DAGs (deterministic layer); and domain experts author ``Skills'': markdown documents encoding vocabulary mappings, parameter constraints, and optimization strategies (knowledge layer). This decomposition confines LLM non-determinism to intent extraction: identical intents always yield identical workflows. We implement and evaluate the architecture on the 1000 Genomes population genetics workflow and Hyperflow WMS running on Kubernetes. In an ablation study on 150 queries, Skills raise full-match intent accuracy from 44% to 83%; skill-driven deferred workflow generation reduces data transfer by 92\%; and the end-to-end pipeline completes queries on Kubernetes with LLM overhead below 15 seconds and cost under $0.001 per query.

AI.tldr: Agentic architecture bridges the gap between scientific questions and workflow execution by using LLMs for intent extraction, deterministic workflow generation, and domain-specific Skills for knowledge encoding, achieving significant improvements in accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current scientific workflow systems automate execution but not the semantic translation from research questions to workflow specifications, requiring manual conversion by scientists with both domain and infrastructure expertise. This creates a barrier between scientific intent and computational execution.
AI.method: Three-layer agentic architecture: 1) Semantic layer - LLM interprets natural language into structured intents, 2) Deterministic layer - validated generators produce reproducible workflow DAGs, 3) Knowledge layer - domain experts author "Skills" (markdown documents encoding vocabulary mappings, parameter constraints, optimization strategies). This confines LLM non-determinism only to intent extraction.
AI.result: Skills improve full-match intent accuracy from 44% to 83%; skill-driven deferred workflow generation reduces data transfer by 92%; end-to-end pipeline completes queries on Kubernetes with LLM overhead under 15 seconds and cost under $0.001 per query. Evaluation conducted on 1000 Genomes population genetics workflow and Hyperflow WMS with 150 queries ablation study.
AI.conclusion: The proposed agentic architecture successfully bridges the semantic gap in scientific workflow systems by decomposing the problem into intent extraction (LLM), deterministic workflow generation, and domain-specific knowledge encoding (Skills). This approach maintains reproducibility while leveraging LLMs' natural language understanding, making scientific workflows more accessible and efficient.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Coupled Demand Forecasting and Supply Chain Optimization
comment: The paper is accepted in the Computers, Materials & Continua journal
summary: Supply chain resilience and efficiency are vital in industries characterized by volatile demand and uncertain supply, such as textiles and personal protective equipment (PPE). Traditional forecasting and optimization approaches often operate in isolation, limiting their real-world effectiveness. This paper proposes a Hybrid AI Framework for Demand-Supply Forecasting and Optimization (HAF-DS), which integrates a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based demand forecasting module with a mixed integer linear programming (MILP) optimization layer. The LSTM captures temporal and contextual demand dependencies, while the optimization layer prescribes cost-efficient replenishment and allocation decisions. The framework jointly minimizes forecasting error and operational cost through embedding-based feature representation and recurrent neural architectures. Experiments on textile sales and supply chain datasets show significant performance gains over statistical and deep learning baselines. On the combined dataset, HAF-DS reduced Mean Absolute Error (MAE) from 15.04 to 12.83 (14.7%), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) from 19.53 to 17.11 (12.4%), and Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) from 9.5% to 8.1%. Inventory cost decreased by 5.4%, stockouts by 27.5%, and service level rose from 95.5% to 97.8%. These results confirm that coupling predictive forecasting with prescriptive optimization enhances both accuracy and efficiency, providing a scalable and adaptable solution for modern textile and PPE supply chains.

AI.tldr: A Hybrid AI Framework (HAF-DS) integrates LSTM-based demand forecasting with MILP optimization for textile/PPE supply chains, significantly improving both prediction accuracy and operational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Supply chains in volatile industries like textiles and PPE face challenges with traditional isolated forecasting and optimization approaches that limit real-world effectiveness, necessitating integrated solutions.
AI.method: HAF-DS combines Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) neural networks for demand forecasting (capturing temporal/contextual dependencies) with mixed integer linear programming (MILP) for optimization, using embedding-based feature representation and recurrent neural architectures to jointly minimize forecasting error and operational costs.
AI.result: Significant improvements: forecasting errors reduced (MAE 14.7%, RMSE 12.4%, MAPE from 9.5% to 8.1%); operational metrics improved (inventory cost down 5.4%, stockouts down 27.5%, service level increased from 95.5% to 97.8%).
AI.conclusion: Coupling predictive forecasting with prescriptive optimization enhances both accuracy and efficiency, providing a scalable, adaptable solution for modern textile and PPE supply chains.

title: Promoting Simple Agents: Ensemble Methods for Event-Log Prediction
summary: We compare lightweight automata-based models (n-grams) with neural architectures (LSTM, Transformer) for next-activity prediction in streaming event logs. Experiments on synthetic patterns and five real-world process mining datasets show that n-grams with appropriate context windows achieve comparable accuracy to neural models while requiring substantially fewer resources. Unlike windowed neural architectures, which show unstable performance patterns, n-grams provide stable and consistent accuracy. While we demonstrate that classical ensemble methods like voting improve n-gram performance, they require running many agents in parallel during inference, increasing memory consumption and latency. We propose an ensemble method, the promotion algorithm, that dynamically selects between two active models during inference, reducing overhead compared to classical voting schemes. On real-world datasets, these ensembles match or exceed the accuracy of non-windowed neural models with lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: Lightweight n-grams match neural models (LSTM/Transformer) for next-activity prediction with fewer resources, plus new ensemble method reduces overhead while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Neural architectures for next-activity prediction in streaming event logs require substantial computational resources and show unstable performance patterns, while lightweight alternatives need exploration.
AI.method: Compares n-grams with LSTM and Transformer models; introduces promotion algorithm ensemble that dynamically selects between two active models during inference to reduce overhead vs classical voting.
AI.result: N-grams with appropriate context windows achieve comparable accuracy to neural models with fewer resources, are more stable; ensembles match/exceed non-windowed neural model accuracy with lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight automata-based models (n-grams) are viable alternatives to neural architectures for next-activity prediction, offering comparable accuracy with better stability and resource efficiency, enhanced further by the proposed promotion ensemble method.

title: Geometric Characterisation and Structured Trajectory Surrogates for Clinical Dataset Condensation
summary: Dataset condensation constructs compact synthetic datasets that retain the training utility of large real-world datasets, enabling efficient model development and potentially supporting downstream research in governed domains such as healthcare. Trajectory matching (TM) is a widely used condensation approach that supervises synthetic data using changes in model parameters observed during training on real data, yet the structure of this supervision signal remains poorly understood. In this paper, we provide a geometric characterisation of trajectory matching, showing that a fixed synthetic dataset can only reproduce a limited span of such training-induced parameter changes. When the resulting supervision signal is spectrally broad, this creates a conditional representability bottleneck. Motivated by this mismatch, we propose Bezier Trajectory Matching (BTM), which replaces SGD trajectories with quadratic Bezier trajectory surrogates between initial and final model states. These surrogates are optimised to reduce average loss along the path while replacing broad SGD-derived supervision with a more structured, lower-rank signal that is better aligned with the optimisation constraints of a fixed synthetic dataset, and they substantially reduce trajectory storage. Experiments on five clinical datasets demonstrate that BTM consistently matches or improves upon standard trajectory matching, with the largest gains in low-prevalence and low-synthetic-budget settings. These results indicate that effective trajectory matching depends on structuring the supervision signal rather than reproducing stochastic optimisation paths.

AI.tldr: Bezier Trajectory Matching (BTM) improves dataset condensation by replacing stochastic gradient descent trajectories with optimized Bezier curves, creating more structured supervision signals that better align with synthetic data optimization constraints while reducing storage requirements.
AI.motivation: Current trajectory matching (TM) for dataset condensation uses SGD training trajectories as supervision, but these signals are spectrally broad and create representability bottlenecks. There's a mismatch between the complex SGD-derived supervision signals and what fixed synthetic datasets can effectively reproduce, limiting condensation performance.
AI.method: Proposes Bezier Trajectory Matching (BTM), which replaces actual SGD trajectories with quadratic Bezier curve surrogates between initial and final model states. These curves are optimized to minimize average loss along the path, creating lower-rank, more structured supervision signals that better match synthetic dataset constraints while substantially reducing trajectory storage requirements.
AI.result: Experiments on five clinical datasets show BTM consistently matches or outperforms standard trajectory matching, with the largest improvements in low-prevalence (imbalanced) and low-synthetic-budget scenarios. The method significantly reduces trajectory storage needs while improving condensation performance.
AI.conclusion: Effective trajectory matching depends on structuring the supervision signal to match synthetic dataset optimization constraints rather than attempting to reproduce stochastic optimization paths. BTM demonstrates that more structured, lower-rank signals are better suited for dataset condensation, particularly in resource-constrained and imbalanced data scenarios common in domains like healthcare.

title: Task-specific Subnetwork Discovery in Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Underwater Navigation
comment: To be published in IEEE OCEANS 2026 (Sanya) conference proceedings
summary: Autonomous underwater vehicles are required to perform multiple tasks adaptively and in an explainable manner under dynamic, uncertain conditions and limited sensing, challenges that classical controllers struggle to address. This demands robust, generalizable, and inherently interpretable control policies for reliable long-term monitoring. Reinforcement learning, particularly multi-task RL, overcomes these limitations by leveraging shared representations to enable efficient adaptation across tasks and environments. However, while such policies show promising results in simulation and controlled experiments, they yet remain opaque and offer limited insight into the agent's internal decision-making, creating gaps in transparency, trust, and safety that hinder real-world deployment. The internal policy structure and task-specific specialization remain poorly understood. To address these gaps, we analyze the internal structure of a pretrained multi-task reinforcement learning network in the HoloOcean simulator for underwater navigation by identifying and comparing task-specific subnetworks responsible for navigating toward different species. We find that in a contextual multi-task reinforcement learning setting with related tasks, the network uses only about 1.5% of its weights to differentiate between tasks. Of these, approximately 85% connect the context-variable nodes in the input layer to the next hidden layer, highlighting the importance of context variables in such settings. Our approach provides insights into shared and specialized network components, useful for efficient model editing, transfer learning, and continual learning for underwater monitoring through a contextual multi-task reinforcement learning method.

AI.tldr: Researchers analyze internal structure of multi-task RL networks for underwater navigation, finding only 1.5% of weights differentiate between related tasks, with 85% connecting context variables to hidden layers.
AI.motivation: Autonomous underwater vehicles need adaptive, explainable control policies for dynamic conditions, but current RL policies remain opaque with limited insight into internal decision-making, creating safety and trust gaps that hinder real-world deployment.
AI.method: Analyze internal structure of pretrained multi-task RL network in HoloOcean simulator for underwater navigation by identifying and comparing task-specific subnetworks responsible for navigating toward different species in a contextual multi-task RL setting.
AI.result: Network uses only ~1.5% of weights to differentiate between related tasks, with ~85% of these connecting context-variable nodes in input layer to next hidden layer, highlighting importance of context variables.
AI.conclusion: Approach provides insights into shared and specialized network components useful for efficient model editing, transfer learning, and continual learning for underwater monitoring through contextual multi-task RL.

title: Large-Scale Data Parallelization of Product Quantization and Inverted Indexing Using Dask
comment: To be published in the CSCE 2022 proceedings
summary: Large-scale Nearest Neighbor (NN) search, though widely utilized in the similarity search field, remains challenged by the computational limitations inherent in processing large scale data. In an effort to decrease the computational expense needed, Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search is often used in applications that do not require the exact similarity search, but instead can rely on an approximation. Product Quantization (PQ) is a memory-efficient ANN effective for clustering all sizes of datasets. Clustering large-scale, high dimensional data requires a heavy computational expense, in both memory-cost and execution time. This work focuses on a unique way to divide and conquer the large scale data in Python using PQ, Inverted Indexing and Dask, combining the results without compromising the accuracy and reducing computational requirements to the level required when using medium-scale data.

AI.tldr: A novel approach combining Product Quantization, Inverted Indexing, and Dask for efficient large-scale approximate nearest neighbor search that reduces computational requirements to medium-scale levels while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Large-scale nearest neighbor search faces computational challenges in memory and execution time, especially for high-dimensional data. Traditional exact search becomes impractical, creating a need for efficient approximate methods that can handle massive datasets.
AI.method: The paper proposes a divide-and-conquer approach in Python combining Product Quantization (PQ) for memory-efficient clustering, Inverted Indexing for efficient retrieval, and Dask for parallel/distributed computing to handle large-scale data partitioning and processing.
AI.result: The method successfully reduces computational requirements (both memory and execution time) to the level typically needed for medium-scale data while maintaining accuracy, enabling efficient processing of large-scale, high-dimensional datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed combination of PQ, Inverted Indexing, and Dask provides an effective solution for large-scale ANN search, overcoming computational limitations through intelligent partitioning and parallel processing without compromising search accuracy.

title: Dilated CNNs for Periodic Signal Processing: A Low-Complexity Approach
comment: 16 pages, 8 figures, the use of deep learning in IoT devices
summary: Denoising of periodic signals and accurate waveform estimation are core tasks across many signal processing domains, including speech, music, medical diagnostics, radio, and sonar. Although deep learning methods have recently shown performance improvements over classical approaches, they require substantial computational resources and are usually trained separately for each signal observation. This study proposes a computationally efficient method based on DCNN and Re-sampling, termed R-DCNN, designed for operation under strict power and resource constraints. The approach targets signals with varying fundamental frequencies and requires only a single observation for training. It generalizes to additional signals via a lightweight resampling step that aligns time scales in signals with different frequencies to re-use the same network weights. Despite its low computational complexity, R-DCNN achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art classical methods, such as autoregressive (AR)-based techniques, as well as conventional DCNNs trained individually for each observation. This combination of efficiency and performance makes the proposed method particularly well suited for deployment in resource-constrained environments without sacrificing denoising or estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: R-DCNN: A computationally efficient denoising method using DCNN with resampling that works with varying fundamental frequencies, requires only single observation training, and achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods while being suitable for resource-constrained environments.
AI.motivation: Denoising periodic signals and accurate waveform estimation are critical tasks across many domains (speech, music, medical diagnostics, radio, sonar). Deep learning methods show performance improvements but require substantial computational resources and separate training for each signal observation, making them impractical for resource-constrained environments.
AI.method: Proposes R-DCNN (DCNN with Re-sampling) - a computationally efficient method using deep convolutional neural network with resampling. It targets signals with varying fundamental frequencies, requires only a single observation for training, and generalizes to additional signals via lightweight resampling that aligns time scales for different frequencies to reuse the same network weights.
AI.result: Despite low computational complexity, R-DCNN achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art classical methods (like autoregressive-based techniques) and conventional DCNNs trained individually for each observation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method combines efficiency and performance, making it particularly well-suited for deployment in resource-constrained environments without sacrificing denoising or estimation accuracy.

title: Transferable SCF-Acceleration through Solver-Aligned Initialization Learning
summary: Machine learning methods that predict initial guesses from molecular geometry can reduce this cost, but matrix-prediction models fail when extrapolating to larger molecules, degrading rather than accelerating convergence [Liu et al. 2025]. We show that this failure is a supervision problem, not an extrapolation problem: models trained on ground-state targets fit those targets well out of distribution, yet produce initial guesses that slow convergence. Solver-Aligned Initialization Learning (SAIL) resolves this for both Hamiltonian and density matrix models by differentiating through the SCF solver end-to-end. We introduce the Effective Relative Iteration Count (ERIC), a correction to the commonly used RIC that accounts for hidden Fock-build overhead. On QM40, containing molecules up to 4$\times$ larger than the training distribution, SAIL reduces ERIC by 37% (PBE), 33% (SCAN), and 27% (B3LYP), more than doubling the previous state-of-the-art reduction on B3LYP (10%). On QMugs molecules 10$\times$ the training size, SAIL delivers a 1.25$\times$ wall-time speedup at the hybrid level of theory, extending ML SCF acceleration to large drug-like molecules.

AI.tldr: SAIL (Solver-Aligned Initialization Learning) is a machine learning method that improves SCF convergence for large molecules by end-to-end training through the SCF solver, addressing supervision problems in previous matrix-prediction models.
AI.motivation: Existing machine learning methods for predicting initial guesses from molecular geometry fail when extrapolating to larger molecules, degrading rather than accelerating SCF convergence. Previous models trained on ground-state targets perform well out of distribution but produce initial guesses that slow convergence.
AI.method: SAIL resolves this by differentiating through the SCF solver end-to-end, aligning learning with convergence behavior rather than just target fitting. The method introduces Effective Relative Iteration Count (ERIC), a correction to Relative Iteration Count that accounts for hidden Fock-build overhead. Applied to both Hamiltonian and density matrix models.
AI.result: On QM40 with molecules up to 4× larger than training distribution: SAIL reduces ERIC by 37% (PBE), 33% (SCAN), and 27% (B3LYP), more than doubling previous state-of-the-art reduction on B3LYP (10%). On QMugs molecules 10× training size: SAIL delivers 1.25× wall-time speedup at hybrid level of theory.
AI.conclusion: SAIL successfully extends ML SCF acceleration to large drug-like molecules by addressing the supervision problem in previous models through end-to-end training aligned with solver convergence behavior, providing significant improvements in both iteration count and actual wall-time performance.

title: Geometric Monomial (GEM): a family of rational 2N-differentiable activation functions
summary: The choice of activation function plays a crucial role in the optimization and performance of deep neural networks. While the Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU) remains the dominant choice due to its simplicity and effectiveness, its lack of smoothness may hinder gradient-based optimization in deep architectures. In this work we propose a family of $C^{2N}$-smooth activation functions whose gate follows a log-logistic CDF, achieving ReLU-like performance with purely rational arithmetic. We introduce three variants: GEM (the base family), E-GEM (an $ε$-parameterized generalization enabling arbitrary $L^p$-approximation of ReLU), and SE-GEM (a piecewise variant eliminating dead neurons with $C^{2N}$ junction smoothness). An $N$-ablation study establishes $N=1$ as optimal for standard-depth networks, reducing the GELU deficit on CIFAR-100 + ResNet-56 from 6.10% to 2.12%. The smoothness parameter $N$ further reveals a CNN-transformer tradeoff: $N=1$ is preferred for deep CNNs, while $N=2$ is preferred for transformers. On MNIST, E-GEM ties the best baseline (99.23%). On CIFAR-10 + ResNet-56, SE-GEM ($ε=10^{-4}$) surpasses GELU (92.51% vs 92.44%) -- the first GEM-family activation to outperform GELU. On CIFAR-100 + ResNet-56, E-GEM reduces the GELU deficit from 6.10% (GEM $N=2$) to just 0.62%. On GPT-2 (124M), GEM achieves the lowest perplexity (72.57 vs 73.76 for GELU), with GEM $N=1$ also beating GELU (73.32). On BERT-small, E-GEM ($ε=10$) achieves the best validation loss (6.656) across all activations. The $ε$-parameterization reveals a scale-dependent optimum: small $ε$ ($10^{-4}$--$10^{-6}$) for deep CNNs and larger transformers, with the special case of small transformers (BERT-small) benefiting from large $ε$ ($ε=10$) due to its limited depth and unconstrained gradients.

AI.tldr: A family of smooth activation functions (GEM variants) that achieve ReLU-like performance using rational arithmetic, with optimal smoothness varying by architecture type.
AI.motivation: ReLU's lack of smoothness may hinder gradient-based optimization in deep networks, creating a need for smooth activation functions that maintain ReLU's effectiveness while enabling better optimization properties.
AI.method: Introduces a family of C^{2N}-smooth activation functions based on log-logistic CDF with three variants: GEM (base family), E-GEM (ε-parameterized generalization for arbitrary L^p-approximation of ReLU), and SE-GEM (piecewise variant eliminating dead neurons). Uses purely rational arithmetic.
AI.result: GEM N=1 reduces GELU deficit on CIFAR-100 + ResNet-56 from 6.10% to 2.12%. E-GEM reduces deficit to 0.62%. SE-GEM outperforms GELU on CIFAR-10 (92.51% vs 92.44%). On GPT-2, GEM achieves lower perplexity than GELU (72.57 vs 73.76). On BERT-small, E-GEM achieves best validation loss (6.656). Optimal smoothness varies: N=1 for deep CNNs, N=2 for transformers, with ε-scale dependence.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GEM family provides smooth, rational alternatives to ReLU with architecture-dependent optimal parameters, enabling ReLU-like performance while maintaining mathematical properties beneficial for optimization.

title: Evaluating Post-hoc Explanations of the Transformer-based Genome Language Model DNABERT-2
comment: Accepted at the 4th World Conference on Explainable Artificial Intelligence, XAI-2026
summary: Explaining deep neural network predictions on genome sequences enables biological insight and hypothesis generation-often of greater interest than predictive performance alone. While explanations of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have been shown to capture relevant patterns in genome sequences, it is unclear whether this transfers to more expressive Transformer-based genome language models (gLMs). To answer this question, we adapt AttnLRP, an extension of layer-wise relevance propagation to the attention mechanism, and apply it to the state-of-the-art gLM DNABERT-2. Thereby, we propose strategies to transfer explanations from token and nucleotide level. We evaluate the adaption of AttnLRP on genomic datasets using multiple metrics. Further, we provide an extensive comparison between the explanations of DNABERT-2 and a baseline CNN. Our results demonstrate that AttnLRP yields reliable explanations corresponding to known biological patterns. Hence, like CNNs, gLMs can also help derive biological insights. This work contributes to the explainability of gLMs and addresses the comparability of relevance attributions across different architectures.

AI.tldr: Researchers adapt AttnLRP to explain Transformer-based genome language models (DNABERT-2) and show these explanations capture biological patterns comparable to CNN explanations.
AI.motivation: While CNN explanations for genome sequences have been shown to capture relevant biological patterns, it's unclear whether this transfers to more expressive Transformer-based genome language models (gLMs). Researchers want to determine if gLMs can also provide biologically meaningful explanations for genomic predictions.
AI.method: The researchers adapt AttnLRP (an extension of layer-wise relevance propagation to attention mechanisms) for DNABERT-2, propose strategies to transfer explanations from token to nucleotide level, evaluate the adaptation using multiple metrics, and compare explanations between DNABERT-2 and baseline CNNs.
AI.result: AttnLRP yields reliable explanations that correspond to known biological patterns in genomic datasets. The explanations from DNABERT-2 are comparable to those from CNNs, demonstrating that gLMs can also help derive biological insights.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based genome language models can provide biologically meaningful explanations similar to CNNs. This work advances gLM explainability and addresses comparability of relevance attributions across different neural network architectures for genomics.

title: Towards Universal Tabular Embeddings: A Benchmark Across Data Tasks
summary: Tabular foundation models aim to learn universal representations of tabular data that transfer across tasks and domains, enabling applications such as table retrieval, semantic search and table-based prediction. Despite the growing number of such models, it remains unclear which approach works best in practice, as existing methods are often evaluated under task-specific settings that make direct comparison difficult. To address this, we introduce TEmBed, the Tabular Embedding Test Bed, a comprehensive benchmark for systematically evaluating tabular embeddings across four representation levels: cell, row, column, and table. Evaluating a diverse set of tabular representation learning models, we show that which model to use depends on the task and representation level. Our results offer practical guidance for selecting tabular embeddings in real-world applications and lay the groundwork for developing more general-purpose tabular representation models.

AI.tldr: TEmBed is a benchmark for evaluating tabular foundation models across different representation levels (cell, row, column, table), helping users select appropriate embeddings based on task requirements.
AI.motivation: Current tabular foundation models are difficult to compare because they're evaluated under task-specific settings with no standardized benchmark. There's a need for systematic evaluation to understand which approaches work best for different tasks and representation levels.
AI.method: The authors introduce TEmBed (Tabular Embedding Test Bed), a comprehensive benchmark that evaluates tabular representation learning models across four representation levels: cell-level, row-level, column-level, and table-level embeddings.
AI.result: Evaluation of diverse tabular representation learning models shows that the best model depends on both the task and the representation level, with no single model performing optimally across all scenarios.
AI.conclusion: TEmBench provides practical guidance for selecting tabular embeddings in real applications and establishes a foundation for developing more general-purpose tabular representation models through standardized evaluation.

title: Fairness under uncertainty in sequential decisions
comment: ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency, 2026
summary:
Fair machine learning (ML) methods help identify and mitigate the risk that algorithms encode or automate social injustices. Algorithmic approaches alone cannot resolve structural inequalities, but they can support socio-technical decision systems by surfacing discriminatory biases, clarifying trade-offs, and enabling governance. Although fairness is well studied in supervised learning, many real ML applications are online and sequential, with prior decisions informing future ones. Each decision is taken under uncertainty due to unobserved counterfactuals and finite samples, with dire consequences for under-represented groups, systematically under-observed due to historical exclusion and selective feedback. A bank cannot know whether a denied loan would have been repaid, and may have less data on marginalized populations.
  This paper introduces a taxonomy of uncertainty in sequential decision-making -- model, feedback, and prediction uncertainty -- providing shared vocabulary for assessing systems where uncertainty is unevenly distributed across groups. We formalize model and feedback uncertainty via counterfactual logic and reinforcement learning, and illustrate harms to decision makers (unrealized gains/losses) and subjects (compounding exclusion, reduced access) of policies that ignore the unobserved space. Algorithmic examples show it is possible to reduce outcome variance for disadvantaged groups while preserving institutional objectives (e.g. expected utility). Experiments on data simulated with varying bias show how unequal uncertainty and selective feedback produce disparities, and how uncertainty-aware exploration alters fairness metrics. The framework equips practitioners to diagnose, audit, and govern fairness risks. Where uncertainty drives unfairness rather than incidental noise, accounting for it is essential to fair and effective decision-making.

AI.tldr: A framework for analyzing fairness in sequential decision-making under uncertainty, addressing how unequal uncertainty distribution across groups creates disparities, with methods to reduce outcome variance while preserving objectives.
AI.motivation: Fair ML methods address social injustice in algorithms, but most fairness research focuses on supervised learning while many real-world applications are sequential/online where prior decisions affect future ones. Uncertainty in decision-making (due to unobserved counterfactuals, finite samples) disproportionately harms underrepresented groups who are systematically under-observed due to historical exclusion and selective feedback, requiring new approaches for sequential settings.
AI.method: Introduces taxonomy of uncertainty in sequential decision-making: model, feedback, and prediction uncertainty. Formalizes model and feedback uncertainty via counterfactual logic and reinforcement learning. Shows algorithmic approaches to reduce outcome variance for disadvantaged groups while preserving institutional objectives (expected utility). Experiments with data simulated with varying bias to examine how unequal uncertainty and selective feedback produce disparities.
AI.result: Shows how unequal uncertainty distribution and selective feedback produce disparities. Demonstrates uncertainty-aware exploration alters fairness metrics. Provides examples where reducing outcome variance for disadvantaged groups while preserving institutional objectives is possible. The framework enables diagnosis, audit, and governance of fairness risks in sequential decision systems.
AI.conclusion: When uncertainty drives unfairness rather than incidental noise, accounting for it is essential for fair and effective decision-making. The framework equips practitioners to assess systems where uncertainty is unevenly distributed across groups, supporting more equitable sequential decision processes in real-world applications.

title: Transferable Physics-Informed Representations via Closed-Form Head Adaptation
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have garnered significant interest for their potential in solving partial differential equations (PDEs) that govern a wide range of physical phenomena. By incorporating physical laws into the learning process, PINN models have demonstrated the ability to learn physical outcomes reasonably well. However, current PINN approaches struggle to predict or solve new PDEs effectively when there is a lack of training examples, indicating they do not generalize well to unseen problem instances. In this paper, we present a transferable learning approach for PINNs premised on a fast Pseudoinverse PINN framework (Pi-PINN). Pi-PINN learns a transferable physics-informed representation in a shared embedding space and enables rapid solving of both known and unknown PDE instances via closed-form head adaptation using a least-squares-optimal pseudoinverse under PDE constraints. We further investigate the synergies between data-driven multi-task learning loss and physics-informed loss, providing insights into the design of more performant PINNs. We demonstrate the effectiveness of Pi-PINN on various PDE problems, including Poisson's equation, Helmholtz equation, and Burgers' equation, achieving fast and accurate physics-informed solutions without requiring any data for unseen instances. Pi-PINN can produce predictions 100-1000 times faster than a typical PINN, while producing predictions with 10-100 times lower relative error than a typical data-driven model even with only two training samples. Overall, our findings highlight the potential of transferable representations with closed-form head adaptation to enhance the efficiency and generalization of PINNs across PDE families and scientific and engineering applications.

AI.tldr: Pi-PINN: A transferable physics-informed neural network framework that learns shared physics-informed representations and enables rapid solving of both known and unknown PDEs via closed-form head adaptation using pseudoinverse optimization.
AI.motivation: Current PINNs struggle to generalize to unseen PDE problems with limited training data. There's a need for PINNs that can effectively transfer knowledge between different PDE instances without requiring extensive retraining or data.
AI.method: Proposes Pi-PINN (Pseudoinverse PINN) framework that learns transferable physics-informed representations in a shared embedding space. Uses closed-form head adaptation via least-squares-optimal pseudoinverse under PDE constraints for rapid solving of both known and unknown PDE instances.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness on Poisson's equation, Helmholtz equation, and Burgers' equation. Pi-PINN achieves 100-1000x faster predictions than typical PINNs and 10-100x lower relative error than typical data-driven models with only two training samples, requiring no data for unseen instances.
AI.conclusion: Transferable representations with closed-form head adaptation significantly enhance PINN efficiency and generalization across PDE families, enabling fast and accurate physics-informed solutions without data for unseen problem instances.

title: PrismaDV: Automated Task-Aware Data Unit Test Generation
summary:
Data is a central resource for modern enterprises, and data validation is essential for ensuring the reliability of downstream applications. However, existing automated data unit testing frameworks are largely task-agnostic: they validate datasets without considering the semantics and requirements of the code that consumes the data.
  We present PrismaDV, a compound AI system that analyzes downstream task code together with dataset profiles to identify data access patterns, infer implicit data assumptions, and generate task-aware executable data unit tests. To further adapt the data unit tests over time to specific datasets and downstream tasks, we propose "Selective Informative Feedback for Task Adaptation" (SIFTA), a prompt-optimization framework that leverages the scarce outcomes from the execution of data unit tests and downstream tasks. We evaluate PrismaDV on two new benchmarks spanning 60 tasks across five datasets, where it consistently outperforms both task-agnostic and task-aware baselines in generating unit tests that reflect the end-to-end impact of data errors. Furthermore, we show that with SIFTA, we can automatically learn prompts for PrismaDV's modules that outperform prompts written by hand or generated from a generic prompt optimizer. We publicly release our benchmarks and prototype implementation.

AI.tldr: PrismaDV is a compound AI system that generates task-aware data unit tests by analyzing downstream code and dataset profiles, with SIFTA for adaptive prompt optimization.
AI.motivation: Existing automated data unit testing frameworks are task-agnostic, validating datasets without considering the semantics and requirements of the downstream code that consumes the data, leading to incomplete validation.
AI.method: PrismaDV analyzes downstream task code together with dataset profiles to identify data access patterns and infer implicit data assumptions, generating task-aware executable data unit tests. SIFTA (Selective Informative Feedback for Task Adaptation) is a prompt-optimization framework that leverages execution outcomes to adapt tests over time.
AI.result: PrismaDV outperforms both task-agnostic and task-aware baselines on benchmarks spanning 60 tasks across five datasets. With SIFTA, learned prompts outperform manually written prompts or those from generic prompt optimizers.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware data unit testing with adaptive prompt optimization significantly improves data validation by considering downstream code semantics, leading to better detection of data errors that impact end-to-end applications.

title: An effective variant of the Hartigan $k$-means algorithm
summary: The k-means problem is perhaps the classical clustering problem and often synonymous with Lloyd's algorithm (1957). It has become clear that Hartigan's algorithm (1975) gives better results in almost all cases, Telgarsky-Vattani note a typical improvement of $5\%$ -- $10\%$. We point out that a very minor variation of Hartigan's method leads to another $2\%$ -- $5\%$ improvement; the improvement tends to become larger when either dimension or $k$ increase.

AI.tldr: A minor variation of Hartigan's k-means clustering algorithm yields 2-5% improvement over Hartigan's method, with greater improvements in higher dimensions or with more clusters.
AI.motivation: Hartigan's algorithm (1975) is known to outperform Lloyd's algorithm (the classic k-means algorithm) by 5-10%, but there's room for further improvement. The authors aim to demonstrate that even a small modification to Hartigan's method can provide significant additional performance gains.
AI.method: Proposes a minor variation/optimization of Hartigan's algorithm for k-means clustering. The exact nature of the variation isn't specified in the abstract, but it's described as "very minor" while producing measurable improvements.
AI.result: The modified algorithm achieves an additional 2-5% improvement over standard Hartigan's method. The improvement increases with either: (1) higher dimensionality of data, or (2) larger number of clusters (k).
AI.conclusion: Even small algorithmic refinements to established clustering methods like Hartigan's algorithm can yield meaningful performance gains, particularly in challenging high-dimensional or large-k scenarios. This suggests that classical algorithms may still have room for optimization.

title: Quotient-Space Diffusion Models
comment: ICLR 2026 Oral Presentation; 40 pages, 5 figures, 6 tables
summary: Diffusion-based generative models have reformed generative AI, and have enabled new capabilities in the science domain, for example, generating 3D structures of molecules. Due to the intrinsic problem structure of certain tasks, there is often a symmetry in the system, which identifies objects that can be converted by a group action as equivalent, hence the target distribution is essentially defined on the quotient space with respect to the group. In this work, we establish a formal framework for diffusion modeling on a general quotient space, and apply it to molecular structure generation which follows the special Euclidean group $\text{SE}(3)$ symmetry. The framework reduces the necessity of learning the component corresponding to the group action, hence simplifies learning difficulty over conventional group-equivariant diffusion models, and the sampler guarantees recovering the target distribution, while heuristic alignment strategies lack proper samplers. The arguments are empirically validated on structure generation for small molecules and proteins, indicating that the principled quotient-space diffusion model provides a new framework that outperforms previous symmetry treatments.

AI.tldr: A principled quotient-space diffusion framework for molecular structure generation that handles SE(3) symmetry by simplifying learning through quotient spaces rather than full group-equivariant models.
AI.motivation: Many scientific tasks have inherent symmetries where objects transformed by group actions are equivalent, requiring distributions defined on quotient spaces. Current group-equivariant diffusion models have unnecessary complexity learning group action components, and heuristic alignment strategies lack proper sampling guarantees.
AI.method: Establishes a formal framework for diffusion modeling on general quotient spaces. For molecular structure generation with SE(3) symmetry, the method reduces the need to learn group action components by working directly on the quotient space, simplifying learning compared to conventional group-equivariant diffusion models.
AI.result: The quotient-space diffusion model provides proper samplers that guarantee recovery of the target distribution (unlike heuristic alignment strategies). Empirical validation on small molecule and protein structure generation shows the framework outperforms previous symmetry treatments.
AI.conclusion: The principled quotient-space diffusion model offers a superior framework for handling symmetries in generative tasks, providing theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements over existing symmetry treatments in molecular structure generation.

title: GFlowState: Visualizing the Training of Generative Flow Networks Beyond the Reward
summary: We present GFlowState, a visual analytics system designed to illuminate the training process of Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets or GFNs). GFlowNets are a probabilistic framework for generating samples proportionally to a reward function. While GFlowNets have proved to be powerful tools in applications such as molecule and material discovery, their training dynamics remain difficult to interpret. Standard machine learning tools allow metric tracking but do not reveal how models explore the sample space, construct sample trajectories, or shift sampling probabilities during training. Our solution, GFlowState, allows users to analyze sampling trajectories, compare the sample space relative to reference datasets, and analyze the training dynamics. To this end, we introduce multiple views, including a chart of candidate rankings, a state projection, a node-link diagram of the trajectory network, and a transition heatmap. These visualizations enable GFlowNet developers and users to investigate sampling behavior and policy evolution, and to identify underexplored regions and sources of training failure. Case studies demonstrate how the system supports debugging and assessing the quality of GFlowNets across application domains. By making the structural dynamics of GFlowNets observable, our work enhances their interpretability and can accelerate GFlowNet development in practice.

AI.tldr: GFlowState: A visual analytics system that visualizes and analyzes the training dynamics of Generative Flow Networks to improve their interpretability and debugging.
AI.motivation: Generative Flow Networks (GFlowNets) are powerful for applications like molecule and material discovery, but their training dynamics are difficult to interpret. Standard machine learning tools only track metrics but don't reveal how models explore sample space, construct trajectories, or shift sampling probabilities during training.
AI.method: Developed GFlowState, a visual analytics system with multiple views: 1) candidate rankings chart, 2) state projection visualization, 3) node-link diagram of trajectory network, and 4) transition heatmap. These visualizations enable analysis of sampling trajectories, comparison with reference datasets, and examination of training dynamics.
AI.result: The system allows GFlowNet developers/users to investigate sampling behavior and policy evolution, identify underexplored regions, and detect sources of training failure. Case studies demonstrate how the system supports debugging and quality assessment across application domains.
AI.conclusion: GFlowState makes the structural dynamics of GFlowNets observable, enhancing their interpretability and potentially accelerating GFlowNet development in practice by enabling better debugging and understanding of training processes.

title: A Scale-Adaptive Framework for Joint Spatiotemporal Super-Resolution with Diffusion Models
summary: Deep-learning video super-resolution has progressed rapidly, but climate applications typically super-resolve (increase resolution) either space or time, and joint spatiotemporal models are often designed for a single pair of super-resolution (SR) factors (upscaling spatial and temporal ratio between the low-resolution sequence and the high-resolution sequence), limiting transfer across spatial resolutions and temporal cadences (frame rates). We present a scale-adaptive framework that reuses the same architecture across factors by decomposing spatiotemporal SR into a deterministic prediction of the conditional mean, with attention, and a residual conditional diffusion model, with an optional mass-conservation (same precipitation amount in inputs and outputs) transform to preserve aggregated totals. Assuming that larger SR factors primarily increase underdetermination (hence required context and residual uncertainty) rather than changing the conditional-mean structure, scale adaptivity is achieved by retuning three factor-dependent hyperparameters before retraining: the diffusion noise schedule amplitude beta (larger for larger factors to increase diversity), the temporal context length L (set to maintain comparable attention horizons across cadences) and optionally a third, the mass-conservation function f (tapered to limit the amplification of extremes for large factors). Demonstrated on reanalysis precipitation over France (Comephore), the same architecture spans super-resolution factors from 1 to 25 in space and 1 to 6 in time, yielding a reusable architecture and tuning recipe for joint spatiotemporal super-resolution across scales.
